EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved south into the region with light precipitation breaking out behind it.  Wintry precipitation increased in intensity as a mid level shortwave trough and entrance region of an upper jet combined to increase lift over the region.  A mix of sleet and freezing rain resulted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter inch of ice accumulation reported in Jal.  Freezing rain on the 5th was followed by sleet the night of the 5th and morning of the 6th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter inch of ice accumulation was reported by the public, 13 miles west of Hobbs.  Precipitation fell as freezing rain, followed by sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed over portions of south central and eastern NM as a potent upper level trough slammed eastward across the region early on the 17th.  Widespread windy conditions were reported with areas across the south central high terrain experiencing wind gusts as high as 70mph. Some damage was also reported around the central and northeast highlands as the potent cold front pushed through the region.  Widespread blowing dust occurred with this event...where some locales reported visibilities below one half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Belen Alexander airport reported peak sustained winds between 40mph and 46mph for nearly 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest to west mid level winds at the base of a passing upper trough resulted in an extended period of high winds in the Guadalupe Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest to west winds occurred in the Guadalupe Mountains in southeast New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry thunderstorm activity with virga produced a dust storm across much of Albuquerque's Westside, Rio Rancho and some central Albuquerque locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS on Albuquerque's west mesa recorded a wind gust of 62 mph near a dry storm. The site also reported 1 mile visibility in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A retired NWS meteorologist observed a thunderstorm producing multiple funnel clouds as it moved about 5 miles northwest of Taos.  No touchdowns were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Retired NWS Meteorologist reported several funnel clouds with a storm 5 miles northwest of Taos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft moved over northern and central New Mexico within the base of a large scale upper level trough over the western United States.  These winds mixed to the surface in localized areas along and immediately east of the northern mountains where high wind gusts were reported during the predawn hours through the early afternoon.  Elsewhere...widespread windy conditions were reported throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Raton ASOS reported sustained winds between 40 and 46 mph throughout the period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread breezy to windy conditions impacted the region as strong winds aloft continued to combine with a surface trough over eastern NM.  An isolated locale over the south central high terrain reported a peak wind gust to 59 mph for an hour or two during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust to 59 mph was reported 5 miles northwest of Nogal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge had become flattened over the region as a result of an upper level disturbance that was over the Texas panhandle.  A surface trough, which provided lift, was across Southeast New Mexico and far West Texas. There was plentiful low-level moisture and instability across the area.  These ingredients allowed severe thunderstorms to develop across the Davis Mountains, Big Bend area, Upper Trans Pecos, and Southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved across Lea County and produced a 60 mph wind gust at the Western Bar and Grill in Gladiola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong west to northwest winds on the backside of a passing upper trough resulted in high winds across portions of southeast New Mexico.

An upper ridge was over northern Mexico, and abundant moisture was over the area from southeast low level winds.  A surface boundary was present across the Permian Basin with upper level disturbances passing nearby which provided lift.   This lift and moisture combined with good instability to produce hail, strong winds, and flash flooding across New Mexico and West Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was over the region with good low level moisture in place. An outflow boundary was present across the Permian Basin and Southeast New Mexico.  This boundary became a focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours.

An upper ridge was over the region with good low level moisture in place. An outflow boundary was present across the Permian Basin and Southeast New Mexico.  This boundary became a focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon and evening hours.

A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranging from 3-10 inches were reported from co-op observers across the county. Sustained wind speeds in the mid forties along with gusts into the fifties were recorded in the county as well. NM DOT closed the main interstate that runs through this county as well as several side roads in the area due to the hazardous blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranging from 3-6 inches were reported from co-op observers across the county. Wind gusts into the fifties were recorded. Some thunder and lightning was reported in this system from observers. NM DOT closed the main interstate that runs through this county as well as several side roads in the area due to the hazardous blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3-6 inches were reported across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge had become flattened over the region as a result of an upper level disturbance that was over the Texas panhandle.  A surface trough, which provided lift, was across Southeast New Mexico and far West Texas. There was plentiful low-level moisture and instability across the area.  These ingredients allowed severe thunderstorms to develop across the Davis Mountains, Big Bend area, Upper Trans Pecos, and Southeast New Mexico.

Strong winds developed over portions of south central and eastern NM as a potent upper level trough slammed eastward across the region early on the 17th.  Widespread windy conditions were reported with areas across the south central high terrain experiencing wind gusts as high as 70mph. Some damage was also reported around the central and northeast highlands as the potent cold front pushed through the region.  Widespread blowing dust occurred with this event...where some locales reported visibilities below one half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The CSBF Transwestern Pump facility reported at peak wind gust to 70mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2013 precipitation was generally below normal with a statewide average of only 40 percent of normal. It was the 29th driest October on record.  The wetter locations in October were across the northern mountains where decent snow fell early in the month,  and again late in the month as a slow-moving storm system was able to draw moisture into New Mexico from early on the 25th into the 26th, providing widespread rain showers and mountain snow showers.  Statewide drought status was virtually unchanged with only slight improvement near the New Mexico-Colorado state line. Approximately 38 percent of the state was classified as severe (D2) drought or worse.

August was quite varied in terms of precipitation, typical of the late monsoon period. Overall, the west was near to above normal, while the east was near to below normal.  The combined areal coverage of severe, extreme and exceptional drought statewide decreased only slightly to 92 percent as compared to 96 percent at the end of July.  The most notable improvement was across parts of northeast New Mexico where the coverage of exceptional drought decreased by nearly one-half.   An unseasonably strong jet stream combined with sufficient moisture to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico early in the month. After a downward trend in thunderstorm activity during the middle part of the month, deep monsoon moisture surged back northward into New Mexico from the 19th through 22nd, then again on the 25th. The 26th through the 28th produced only light to moderate rainfall with no reports of minor or major flooding. The end of the month turned a bit wetter as a disturbance moved up western New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet month for most of Southern New Mexico lead to many areas improving to Severe(D2) drought or better. A late monsoon surge in the middle of the month brought several inches of rain to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone transitioned from Extreme(D3) to Severe(D2) drought on September 17th. The Cloudcroft Cooperative Observer reported 9.54 inches of precipitation which was 6.41 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2013 precipitation was generally below normal with a statewide average of only 40 percent of normal. It was the 29th driest October on record.  The wetter locations in October were across the northern mountains where decent snow fell early in the month,  and again late in the month as a slow-moving storm system was able to draw moisture into New Mexico from early on the 25th into the 26th, providing widespread rain showers and mountain snow showers.  Statewide drought status was virtually unchanged with only slight improvement near the New Mexico-Colorado state line. Approximately 38 percent of the state was classified as severe (D2) drought or worse.

Low level moisture lingering over the state interacted with a weak upper level disturbance and produced heavy rainfall and isolated large hail over portions of northern New Mexico.  Nickel size hail was reported near Raton along with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported at the Raton Fire Department between 1250pm and 1259pm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Funnel cloud spotted by truck driver on Interstate 25.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very wet month for most of Southern New Mexico lead to many areas improving to Severe(D2) drought or better. A late monsoon surge in the middle of the month brought several inches of rain to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone started out in Extreme(D3) drought and transitioned to Severe(D2) drought on September 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moisture plume was centered on the Arizona-New Mexico border region with surface dew points around 60F. An upper level trough was located across Western Arizona providing lift and a continuous tap of moisture from the Baja region. Locally heavy rain with rates of over an inch per hour occurred across the Silver City area causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from 911 Dispatch and amateur radio reported serious street flooding between Silver City and Pinos Altos. Reports of at least 5 inches of water were covering streets in downtown Silver City.

August was quite varied in terms of precipitation, typical of the late monsoon period. Overall, the west was near to above normal, while the east was near to below normal.  The combined areal coverage of severe, extreme and exceptional drought statewide decreased only slightly to 92 percent as compared to 96 percent at the end of July.  The most notable improvement was across parts of northeast New Mexico where the coverage of exceptional drought decreased by nearly one-half.   An unseasonably strong jet stream combined with sufficient moisture to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico early in the month. After a downward trend in thunderstorm activity during the middle part of the month, deep monsoon moisture surged back northward into New Mexico from the 19th through 22nd, then again on the 25th. The 26th through the 28th produced only light to moderate rainfall with no reports of minor or major flooding. The end of the month turned a bit wetter as a disturbance moved up western New Mexico.

A persistent area of high pressure over the Eastern United States setup a deep east to southeast flow off the Gulf of Mexico which provided plenty of moisture to Otero County. An upper trough remained in place through New Mexico which provided the lift for very heavy rains to occur over Otero County and especially the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Serious flooding was reported in downtown Alamogordo. Street flooding was also reported in Boles Acres.  Highway 82 out of Alamogordo was also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high setup over the Gila Region trapped low level moisture across the region.  Thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain and with a weak flow in the mid and low levels, storms moved very slowly and produced locally heavy rain over Eastern Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a few homes were evacuated when Cameron Creek came up quickly due to heavy rain. No water got into the homes but did cause local flooding on city streets.

A surface trough was in place across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico which created instability.  An outflow boundary was across the northern Permian Basin in advance of a cold front that was moving southward across West Texas as an upper level disturbance moved over the area.  These features helped to create good lift over the area. A dryline was across the Permian Basin and Lower Trans Pecos with good moisture present.  Thunderstorms developed along the dryline, outflow boundary, and cold front and produced severe wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved over Lea County and produced a 62 mph wind gust two miles southwest of Tatum.

A weak upper low was located over Southeast Arizona with weak south-southwest flow aloft over New Mexico. Ample low level moisture combined with these other features allowed for slow moving thunderstorms to drop heavy rain over Northwestern Sierra County and produce localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported low water crossings along Highway 52 were flowing about 3 to 4 feet deep with a lot of debris. Road north of Winston was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was located near the New Mexico-Mexico border region with weak flow aloft. A moist southeast surface flow beneath this high allowed dew points to remain in the mid to upper 50s across the region. This combination led to ample instability with slow moving storms to produce localized flash flooding in Grant County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Bayard making roads difficult to pass through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low level moisture was located over the Gila region with very weak flow aloft as a broad upper high was located across the western two-thirds of the country. Slow moving storms produced over an inch per hour rainfall rates and localized flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported water over several roads in the eastern portion of Silver City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep low level moisture was located over the Gila region with very weak flow aloft as a broad upper high was located across the western two-thirds of the country. Slow moving storms produced over an inch per hour rainfall rates and localized flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Winston reported almost an inch of rain in less than an hour. Highway 52 was flooded near Winston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deep moisture was trapped under a large upper high which was located across much of the country. Dew points near 60 were recorded near the Arizona-New Mexico border region. Sufficient instability combined with daytime heating to produce very heavy rain across the Lordsburg area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hidalgo County Sheriff reported major flooding in Lordsburg. Four to five feet of standing water were reported at the Interstate 10 under passes near Motel Drive at both the east and west ends of Lordsburg. A vehicle extraction was also performed at the Highway 70 underpass. A mesonet site reported 2.16 inches of rainfall near the Interstate 10 port of entry with instantaneous rainfall rates exceeded 5 inches per hour at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: All areas of Southern New Mexico either had no change or improved in their drought conditions due to continued monsoon rains across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought conditions continued through the month. The Cooperative Observer at Gila Hot Springs reported 2.24 inches of rain which was 0.78 inches below normal.

An upper level low was over northern Arizona, and the circulation around the low brought in low level moisture across southeast New Mexico and West Texas.  Upper level disturbances moved over the region providing additional lift.  This lift and good moisture supply allowed for flash flooding to develop across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Eddy County and produced flash flooding in Carlsbad.  At least one foot of water was running over Tansill Road in south Carlsbad.  The street was barricaded as a result of the flooding.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate since no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door frontal boundary pushed southwest and stalled over the northeast plains of New Mexico.  This boundary interacted with abundant moisture and instability over the area to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms. Some of these thunderstorms were strong to severe with torrential rainfall and hail. Heavy rainfall over a large portion of northern Colfax and Union counties drained through Dry Cimarron Creek and produced a near historical rise on the Cimarron River at Kenton, Oklahoma.  The river gauge reported a peak stage of 21.47 feet and peak discharge of 19,800 cubic feet per second.  This was the 3rd highest crest on record falling behind 21.62 feet on June 25, 1977 and the record of 22.32 feet on October 17, 1965.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State highway 406 between mile marker 18 and 19 is impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak back door frontal boundary pushed southwest and stalled over the northeast plains of New Mexico.  This boundary interacted with abundant moisture and instability over the area to produce numerous showers and thunderstorms. Some of these thunderstorms were strong to severe with torrential rainfall and hail. Heavy rainfall over a large portion of northern Colfax and Union counties drained through Dry Cimarron Creek and produced a near historical rise on the Cimarron River at Kenton, Oklahoma.  The river gauge reported a peak stage of 21.47 feet and peak discharge of 19,800 cubic feet per second.  This was the 3rd highest crest on record falling behind 21.62 feet on June 25, 1977 and the record of 22.32 feet on October 17, 1965.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail 20 miles northeast of Des Moines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance had moved over the area, and moisture was present.  This led to the development of thunderstorms across Southeast New Mexico which resulted in severe wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved over Lea County and produced a 60 mph wind gust in Lovington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October 2013 precipitation was generally below normal with a statewide average of only 40 percent of normal. It was the 29th driest October on record.  The wetter locations in October were across the northern mountains where decent snow fell early in the month,  and again late in the month as a slow-moving storm system was able to draw moisture into New Mexico from early on the 25th into the 26th, providing widespread rain showers and mountain snow showers.  Statewide drought status was virtually unchanged with only slight improvement near the New Mexico-Colorado state line. Approximately 38 percent of the state was classified as severe (D2) drought or worse.

August was quite varied in terms of precipitation, typical of the late monsoon period. Overall, the west was near to above normal, while the east was near to below normal.  The combined areal coverage of severe, extreme and exceptional drought statewide decreased only slightly to 92 percent as compared to 96 percent at the end of July.  The most notable improvement was across parts of northeast New Mexico where the coverage of exceptional drought decreased by nearly one-half.   An unseasonably strong jet stream combined with sufficient moisture to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico early in the month. After a downward trend in thunderstorm activity during the middle part of the month, deep monsoon moisture surged back northward into New Mexico from the 19th through 22nd, then again on the 25th. The 26th through the 28th produced only light to moderate rainfall with no reports of minor or major flooding. The end of the month turned a bit wetter as a disturbance moved up western New Mexico.

August was quite varied in terms of precipitation, typical of the late monsoon period. Overall, the west was near to above normal, while the east was near to below normal.  The combined areal coverage of severe, extreme and exceptional drought statewide decreased only slightly to 92 percent as compared to 96 percent at the end of July.  The most notable improvement was across parts of northeast New Mexico where the coverage of exceptional drought decreased by nearly one-half.   An unseasonably strong jet stream combined with sufficient moisture to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico early in the month. After a downward trend in thunderstorm activity during the middle part of the month, deep monsoon moisture surged back northward into New Mexico from the 19th through 22nd, then again on the 25th. The 26th through the 28th produced only light to moderate rainfall with no reports of minor or major flooding. The end of the month turned a bit wetter as a disturbance moved up western New Mexico.
Most of Southern New Mexico started out the month in Moderate(D1) to Severe(D2) drought, but by the end of another dry month, most locations were in Extreme(D3) to Exceptional(D4) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: THe month started out with Severe(D2) drought across the northern portions of the zone and Moderate(D1) drought south. At the end of the month, the northwestern areas were placed in Exceptional(D4) drought with Extreme(D3) drought over the remainder of the area. The Orogrande Cooperative Observer reported just a trace of precipitation which was 0.28 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front pushed south across the eastern plains and through the central mountain chain and Rio Grande Valley late on the 21st through the 22nd.  Record to near record cold temperatures moved into the area with this arctic air mass.  Strong gap winds developed below canyons in the Rio Grande Valley where gusts near 50 mph were common for several days from Albuquerque south to Socorro and Carrizozo.  Meanwhile, an unseasonably strong, slow-moving upper level low pressure system over southern California provided a deep tap of abundant moisture over New Mexico.  The combination of very cold air at the surface and a slow-moving surge of moisture over the area provided an extended duration of winter weather over the region.  Widespread impacts to travel were reported with several accidents and road closures across the area.  A 4-year old girl was killed in a roll over accident on Highway 70 in Chaves county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were reported around Gallup.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast moving upper level trough and associated potent cold front over the Great Basin interacted with a tap of subtropical moisture advancing north out of Mexico to produce moderate to heavy snowfall over portions of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.  The heaviest snowfall occurred along an intense band that impacted the northern Sangre de Cristos where amounts from 8 to 14 were common early on the 5th.  Widespread significant travel impacts did not occur over the area as temperatures hovered around the freezing mark. A few areas, however, did experience slick road conditions with slushy snowfall accumulations and even a few school delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 14 inches were reported from COOP, SNOTEL, CoCoRaHS, trained spotters, and the public.  This was the first significant snowfall to impact the area this fall season leading to school delays and hazardous travel conditions.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tres Lagunas Fire burned 10,219 acres within and near prized recreation and resource areas. The fire was human-caused when electrical lines went down on May 30, igniting Ponderosa pine and other timbers. The assessment report by the Burned Area Emergency Response team indicated this was a relatively healthy wildfire burn and included a small area of re-burn in the 2002 Viveash Fire. Approximately 15% of the burn was high intensity, 13% moderate, and 72% low. The National Interagency Coordination Center indicated the total cost of this fire will near $3.5 million, including efforts to reduce the effects of monsoonal flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines ignited a wildfire along highway 63 near the vacation community of Tres Lagunas. The wildfire spread from 7,395 acres at the end of May to just under 10,300 acres by June 12th.  The fire did not experience any further growth through early July before being declared 100% contained on July 5th.  Extensive efforts have been put forth to mitigate the impacts from monsoon flooding.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in November 2013 was generally above normal with a statewide average of 0.30 inches above normal, resulting in the 22nd wettest November on record.  However, reported precipitation was variable across the state. Precipitation, mainly in the form of snow, favored the north-central and northwest mountains where significant snow accumulations were recorded during two notable winter storms during the month. The southern tier of the area generally missed out on precipitation resulting in a dry start to the water year.   Severe drought areas (D2 category) were trimmed slightly in northern New Mexico, with the improvements tempered by low reservoir conditions in the northwest quadrant of the state. Overall,  the percent of the state classified as severe or worse drought remained in the 36 to 37 percent range during the month.

Another below average month for precipitation resulted in no change to drought conditoins from October across Southern New Mexico. Severe Drought(D2) remained in place over a small portion of Far Southwest New Mexico and also around the Sacramento Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation in November 2013 was generally above normal with a statewide average of 0.30 inches above normal, resulting in the 22nd wettest November on record.  However, reported precipitation was variable across the state. Precipitation, mainly in the form of snow, favored the north-central and northwest mountains where significant snow accumulations were recorded during two notable winter storms during the month. The southern tier of the area generally missed out on precipitation resulting in a dry start to the water year.   Severe drought areas (D2 category) were trimmed slightly in northern New Mexico, with the improvements tempered by low reservoir conditions in the northwest quadrant of the state. Overall,  the percent of the state classified as severe or worse drought remained in the 36 to 37 percent range during the month.

Moisture was coming into the area from Tropical Storm Octave in the Pacific Ocean. Additional low-level moisture was moving in from the Gulf of Mexico.  A stationary front was present near the Pecos River, and upper level disturbances were moving over the area from the tropical storm providing additional lift.  This good supply of moisture and combined lift helped to create flash flooding in southeast New Mexico and the Permian Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain moved across Lea County and produced flash flooding in Hobbs.  Several street intersections in Hobbs were reported to be flooded with one to one and a half feet of water.  The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An 150kt jet streak was located near Albuquerque at 12z on the 4th with a broad upper trough over the Western United States. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 mph was reported 1 mile southeast of the White Sands Missile Range Main Post. A 61 mph gust was also reported 1 mile west of the Main Post.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold core upper level low moved eastwards across the region. While moisture was rather limited with the storm, the system did display rather impressive dynamics. As the storm passed over the Sacramento mountains, upslope flow managed to help generate a band of snow that accumulated across the higher terrain above 7500 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 7.0 inches of snow at Cloudcroft from overnight snowfall. Additionally, several COCORAHS sites in and around Cloudcroft also reported between 4.0 and 7.0 inches of snowfall during this time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper level storm system with winds aloft in excess of 120 knots slowly worked it's way from southern California eastwards to southern New Mexico and West Texas. Over the course of two days a strong surface pressure gradient developed in association with this system that induced strong southwest and westerly surface winds. In addition the some of the stronger winds aloft mixed down to the surface to enhance the gust potential during this time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 mph wind gust was reported at 12:13 pm at the mesonet site just north of Twin Peaks in Dona Ana county. Later in the afternoon a gust of 63 mph was reported at NWS EPZ around 5:32 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity drastically increased across portions of central and western New Mexico throughout the month of April. ||Only a few storms brought precipitation to the state during the month, and those storms were focused on northern areas of the state.  Percent of normal precipitation was well below normal for northern and central New Mexico. Most locations in eastern and central New Mexico were under 25% of normal. Along the Rio Grande Valley percent of normal inched up to 25-50% of normal. Areas in the west, northwest and north central ranged from 50-90% of normal with some isolated pockets of values at or over 100% mostly in the higher terrain areas along the peaks of mountain ranges.||To start the month off Extreme drought (D3) covered nearly 50% of the state covering most of the eastern plains extending as far west as the west central mountains with some Exceptional drought (D4) speckled across this area. Severe drought (D2) covered the remainder of the area with some Moderate drought (D1) holding on in the San Francisco River Valley and in portions of the far northwest highlands. One week into the month D3 conditions had crept all the way to the western border between AZ and NM in the west central plateau.  Into the latter half of the month D3 conditions had increased to cover 77% of the area with all areas in the northwest returning to D2. By the end of the month with most of the state in D3, D4 conditions increased along central NM in the Rio Grande Valley extending from Bernalillo past Socorro southward. D4 increased from 4% at the beginning of the month to 24% by months end. The far northwest highland zone which was making an attempt at getting out of drought was sucked back into D2 conditions. The San Francisco River Valley zone which was had thus far managed to stay out of drought conditions was no longer successfully and now every zone in the ABQ CWA is experiencing  D2 or higher drought conditions.

A powerful upper level low pressure system that intensified over Colorado forced a potent dry slot and associated strong winds over New Mexico.  Many locations across central and eastern New Mexico reported peak wind gusts up to 60 mph, with a few locations across the eastern plains peaking up to 70 mph. Numerous reports of damage were received across the region.  Widespread blowing dust across the region reduced visibilities below 3 miles, with a few areas of Curry and Roosevelt counties down below one quarter mile for several hours.  Intense crosswinds along a section of railroad near Fort Sumner overturned 30 to 40 cars of a BNSF train carrying cases of tuna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cannon Air Force Base and Melrose Bombing Range reported peak wind gusts between 59 and 66 mph during this period.

Portions of the Rio Grande Valley and eastern plains, for example, recorded near to above normal precipitation.  Areas in eastern Catron County as well as most of Socorro County also experienced near to above normal precipitation. North-central portions of the state, the south-central mountains and areas across the west had 50% or lower percent of normal precipitation for the month.||The coverage of Exceptional Drought (D4) nearly doubled, encompassing most of central New Mexico and the eastern half of the state. At the beginning of the month D4 conditions impacted 25% of the area and by month's end that had increased to 45%.  The remaining drought categories remained nearly the same. The only areas in New Mexico that were not classified as D4 were in the D3 category, or extreme drought, while the the northeast corner and north central areas were in  D2 or severe drought.

The only areas in New Mexico that were not classified as D4 were in the D3 category, or extreme drought, while the the northeast corner and north central areas were in  D2 or severe drought.

A low pressure system over the Pacific northwest made its way toward New Mexico over the weekend bringing major weather changes through mid week. As the system moved into the Four Corners area Monday strong winds were prevalent statewide. As the system continued to trek east and north the winds subsided while precipitation began over western and northern portions of the state. Precipitation developed initially as rain with some thunderstorms Monday afternoon. As temperatures dropped into the overnight hours rain turned over to snow with the New Mexico/Colorado border coming out as the winners for this event. Precipitation tapered off into Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Aprswxnet/CWOP station reported a gust to 61mph during the early afternoon. High sustained speeds were measured as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity drastically increased across portions of central and western New Mexico throughout the month of April. ||Only a few storms brought precipitation to the state during the month, and those storms were focused on northern areas of the state.  Percent of normal precipitation was well below normal for northern and central New Mexico. Most locations in eastern and central New Mexico were under 25% of normal. Along the Rio Grande Valley percent of normal inched up to 25-50% of normal. Areas in the west, northwest and north central ranged from 50-90% of normal with some isolated pockets of values at or over 100% mostly in the higher terrain areas along the peaks of mountain ranges.||To start the month off Extreme drought (D3) covered nearly 50% of the state covering most of the eastern plains extending as far west as the west central mountains with some Exceptional drought (D4) speckled across this area. Severe drought (D2) covered the remainder of the area with some Moderate drought (D1) holding on in the San Francisco River Valley and in portions of the far northwest highlands. One week into the month D3 conditions had crept all the way to the western border between AZ and NM in the west central plateau.  Into the latter half of the month D3 conditions had increased to cover 77% of the area with all areas in the northwest returning to D2. A powerful upper level low pressure system that intensified over Colorado forced a potent dry slot and associated strong winds over New Mexico.  Many locations across central and eastern New Mexico reported peak wind gusts up to 60 mph, with a few locations across the eastern plains peaking up to 70 mph. Numerous reports of damage were received across the region.  Widespread blowing dust across the region reduced visibilities below 3 miles, with a few areas of Curry and Roosevelt counties down below one quarter mile for several hours.  Intense crosswinds along a section of railroad near Fort Sumner overturned 30 to 40 cars of a BNSF train carrying cases of tuna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds reported all day at top of Sandia Peak tramway with peak gust to 71 mph.  Strong winds also reported for much of the day at bottom of Sandia Peak tramway with peak gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge had become flattened over the region as a result of an upper level disturbance that was over the Texas panhandle. These ingredients allowed severe thunderstorms to develop across the Davis Mountains, Big Bend area, Upper Trans Pecos, and Southeast New Mexico.

A downed power line near Sulphur Creek Road in the Jemez Mountains ignited a wildfire that spread into the Valles Caldera National Preserve through much of June. No damage was reported with this fire through the month of June however prized wilderness and conservation areas were torched. The most significant fire growth period occurred during the first week of June when it grew from 1,745 acres to just over 18,000 acres. Early surveys from the Burned Area Emergency Response assessment indicate this was a healthy forest burn and not the recent destructive kinds observed recently over the state. The assessment showed that approximately 74% of the acreage within the fire perimeter were either unburned or received a low-severity burn, 23% sustained a burn of a moderate severity, and approximately 3% burned at a high severity. The total cost of battling this wildfire according to the National Interagency Coordination Center is near $2.7 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed power line along Sulphur Creek Road ignited a wildfire that spread east into the Valles Caldera National Preserve. The fire grew from 653 acres at the end of May to 23,937 acres on June 23rd.  No additional fire growth was reported in early July before 100% containment was declared on July 11th. Significant efforts have been put forth to mitigate the impacts of monsoon flooding.

Widespread blowing dust occurred with this event...where some locales reported visibilities below one half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dora mesonet site reported peak sustained winds between 40mph and 45mph on and off for several hours.

A secondary upper level low pressure system followed quickly behind the initial storm system on February 9 and 10th continuing snowfall over much of New Mexico. A strong cold front moved south over the plains and interacted with this upper wave to focus the second round of snowfall across eastern New Mexico, particularly the far northeast highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total accumulations between 3 and 6 inches were reported from trained spotters, COOP sites, and the public.

By the end of the month with most of the state in D3, D4 conditions increased along central NM in the Rio Grande Valley extending from Bernalillo past Socorro southward. D4 increased from 4% at the beginning of the month to 24% by months end. The far northwest highland zone which was making an attempt at getting out of drought was sucked back into D2 conditions. The San Francisco River Valley zone which was had thus far managed to stay out of drought conditions was no longer successfully and now every zone in the ABQ CWA is experiencing  D2 or higher drought conditions.

A powerful upper level low pressure system that intensified over Colorado forced a potent dry slot and associated strong winds over New Mexico.  Many locations across central and eastern New Mexico reported peak wind gusts up to 60 mph, with a few locations across the eastern plains peaking up to 70 mph. Numerous reports of damage were received across the region.  Widespread blowing dust across the region reduced visibilities below 3 miles, with a few areas of Curry and Roosevelt counties down below one quarter mile for several hours.  Intense crosswinds along a section of railroad near Fort Sumner overturned 30 to 40 cars of a BNSF train carrying cases of tuna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds between 40 and 47 mph reported on and off through the period from Mesonet, RAWS, and other Federal sites across Roosevelt county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity continued to increase across portions of eastern New Mexico throughout the month.||Overall, May fared better than previous months with numerous days of at least some measurable precipitation. North-central portions of the state, the south-central mountains and areas across the west had 50% or lower percent of normal precipitation for the month.||The coverage of Exceptional Drought (D4) nearly doubled, encompassing most of central New Mexico and the eastern half of the state. The only areas in New Mexico that were not classified as D4 were in the D3 category, or extreme drought, while the the northeast corner and north central areas were in  D2 or severe drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines down at intersection of Road 7 and T.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tres Lagunas Fire burned 10,219 acres within and near prized recreation and resource areas. The fire was human-caused when electrical lines went down on May 30, igniting Ponderosa pine and other timbers. The assessment report by the Burned Area Emergency Response team indicated this was a relatively healthy wildfire burn and included a small area of re-burn in the 2002 Viveash Fire. Approximately 15% of the burn was high intensity, 13% moderate, and 72% low. The National Interagency Coordination Center indicated the total cost of this fire will near $3.5 million, including efforts to reduce the effects of monsoonal flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines ignited a wildfire along highway 63 near the vacation community of Tres Lagunas. The wildfire spread from 7,395 acres at the end of May to just under 10,300 acres by June 12th.

A downed power line near Sulphur Creek Road in the Jemez Mountains ignited a wildfire that spread into the Valles Caldera National Preserve through much of June.  No damage was reported with this fire through the month of June however prized wilderness and conservation areas were torched.  The most significant fire growth period occurred during the first week of June when it grew from 1,745 acres to just over 18,000 acres. Early surveys from the Burned Area Emergency Response assessment indicate this was a healthy forest burn and not the recent destructive kinds observed recently over the state. The assessment showed that approximately 74% of the acreage within the fire perimeter were either unburned or received a low-severity burn, 23% sustained a burn of a moderate severity, and approximately 3% burned at a high severity.  The total cost of battling this wildfire according to the National Interagency Coordination Center is near $2.7 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed power line along Sulphur Creek Road ignited a wildfire that spread east into the Valles Caldera National Preserve. The fire grew from 653 acres at the end of May to 23,937 acres on June 23rd.

A large storm system along the west coast crossed central California and southwest Arizona then moved into southwest New Mexico on the 20th. As the system pushed across the southwestern states, snow began falling in northern New Mexico then increased in intensity and coverage across west central New Mexico. Along with the snow, the position of the low as it moved east allowed for gusty winds in south central portions of the state. The system exited the state on the 21st with only a few light showers lingering along the Texas and New Mexico border in northeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 4-10 inches were reported from various sources across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system over the Pacific northwest made its way toward New Mexico over the weekend bringing major weather changes through mid week. As the system moved into the Four Corners area Monday strong winds were prevalent statewide. As the system continued to trek east and north the winds subsided while precipitation began over western and northern portions of the state. Precipitation developed initially as rain with some thunderstorms Monday afternoon. As temperatures dropped into the overnight hours rain turned over to snow with the New Mexico/Colorado border coming out as the winners for this event. Precipitation tapered off into Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners ASOS reported gust to 58mph and sustained winds to 40mph throughout the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dryline over the east central and southeast plains of New Mexico, coupled with deep moisture and instability,  provided the focus for scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.  Large hail was the main impact from these severe thunderstorms within the area from southwest Chaves County northeast across Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail reported near Portales airport.

A back door cold front moved south on the eastern plains and spawned a few severe thunderstorms over Chaves County.  These storms were rare in that they developed during the pre-dawn hours of May 10th and produced large hail and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail began at the Roswell Correctional Facility around 330am and grew to the size of ping pong balls at 340am and remained severe for several minutes afterward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure in place over the state with a thin layer of mid level moisture produced conditions conducive for dry thunderstorms with strong outflow winds.  During the evening hours one of these dry storms produced a dry microburst which toppled nearly one dozen power poles near the Albuquerque Foothills. Another dry microburst near the Albuquerque Metropolitan Detention Center downed a few power lines. Other areas of the city also experienced minor damage from these winds.  Power outages were reported in numerous locations throughout the city with crews continuing to restore power into the following afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry microburst impacted portions of Albuquerque metropolitan area.  ASOS measured peak winds between 40 and 55mph. Power lines were reported damaged near the Albuquerque Metropolitan Detention Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity drastically increased across portions of central and western New Mexico throughout the month of April. ||Only a few storms brought precipitation to the state during the month, and those storms were focused on northern areas of the state.  Percent of normal precipitation was well below normal for northern and central New Mexico. Most locations in eastern and central New Mexico were under 25% of normal. Along the Rio Grande Valley percent of normal inched up to 25-50% of normal. Areas in the west, northwest and north central ranged from 50-90% of normal with some isolated pockets of values at or over 100% mostly in the higher terrain areas along the peaks of mountain ranges.||To start the month off Extreme drought (D3) covered nearly 50% of the state covering most of the eastern plains extending as far west as the west central mountains with some Exceptional drought (D4) speckled across this area. Severe drought (D2) covered the remainder of the area with some Moderate drought (D1) holding on in the San Francisco River Valley and in portions of the far northwest highlands. One week into the month D3 conditions had crept all the way to the western border between AZ and NM in the west central plateau.  Into the latter half of the month D3 conditions had increased to cover 77% of the area with all areas in the northwest returning to D2. By the end of the month with most of the state in D3, D4 conditions increased along central NM in the Rio Grande Valley extending from Bernalillo past Socorro southward. D4 increased from 4% at the beginning of the month to 24% by months end. The far northwest highland zone which was making an attempt at getting out of drought was sucked back into D2 conditions. The San Francisco River Valley zone which was had thus far managed to stay out of drought conditions was no longer successfully and now every zone in the ABQ CWA is experiencing  D2 or higher drought conditions.

A large storm system along the west coast crossed central California and southwest Arizona then moved into southwest New Mexico on the 20th. As the system pushed across the southwestern states, snow began falling in northern New Mexico then increased in intensity and coverage across west central New Mexico. Along with the snow, the position of the low as it moved east allowed for gusty winds in south central portions of the state. The system exited the state on the 21st with only a few light showers lingering along the Texas and New Mexico border in northeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts between 7-13 inches were reported from various sources across the region. Severe driving conditions were also reported on local NM DOT webpage throughout the area during this time.

A low pressure system over the Pacific northwest made its way toward New Mexico over the weekend bringing major weather changes through mid week. As the system moved into the Four Corners area Monday strong winds were prevalent statewide. As the system continued to trek east and north the winds subsided while precipitation began over western and northern portions of the state. As temperatures dropped into the overnight hours rain turned over to snow with the New Mexico/Colorado border coming out as the winners for this event. Precipitation tapered off into Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and SNOTEL sites reported snow amounts ranging from 5-8 inches across the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tres Lagunas Fire burned 10,219 acres within and near prized recreation and resource areas. The fire was human-caused when electrical lines went down on May 30, igniting Ponderosa pine and other timbers. The assessment report by the Burned Area Emergency Response team indicated this was a relatively healthy wildfire burn and included a small area of re-burn in the 2002 Viveash Fire.  Approximately 15% of the burn was high intensity, 13% moderate, and 72% low. The National Interagency Coordination Center indicated the total cost of this fire will near $3.5 million, including efforts to reduce the effects of monsoonal flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines ignited a wildfire along highway 63 near the vacation community of Tres Lagunas.  The wildfire spread quickly to 7,395 acres in the last 2 days of May.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system along the west coast crossed central California and southwest Arizona then moved into southwest New Mexico on the 20th. As the system pushed across the southwestern states, snow began falling in northern New Mexico then increased in intensity and coverage across west central New Mexico. Along with the snow, the position of the low as it moved east allowed for gusty winds in south central portions of the state. The system exited the state on the 21st with only a few light showers lingering along the Texas and New Mexico border in northeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 8-10 inches were reported from various sources across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossed the Four Corners area during the afternoon and exited southeast into west Texas late evening. The associated cold front plunged southward from Colorado and reached the southern tier of New Mexico during the late evening.  Showers and thunderstorms developed over the northeast highlands and adjacent eastern plains as the cold front moved south. Winds were significant across the central and south central mountain region where advisory level sustained wind speeds at or above 30 mph were observed and, in some cases, high wind conditions in the east with gusts near 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cannon Air Force Base reported peak wind of 58 mph.

A well defined upper level shortwave diving south out of the central Rockies interacted with deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Severe outflow winds, localized wind damage, large hail, locally heavy rainfall, and significant blowing dust were reported with some of these storms. The most severe thunderstorms produced baseball size hail around Arabela and damaging wind in excess of 70mph around Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several strong outflow wind gusts in Moriarty between 7pm and 8pm with strongest to 75mph at around 745pm.

A low pressure system centered off the California coast spread precipitation over portions of New Mexico throughout the day on the 8th.  Initial rain bands formed in central and western New Mexico and brought decent amounts of precipitation along with thunder and lightning. Snow began to fall in western and northern high terrain areas of the state into the evening of the 8th.  As the low moved east throughout the overnight hours, by around 11am, the low center was near the Four Corners region. The low began to elongate into the afternoon of the 9th as it ejected out of northeastern Colorado with continued wrap around precipitation affecting northeastern New Mexico through early Sunday morning. This system brought moderate snow to areas of New Mexico along with high winds which caused near white out conditions along the New Mexico/Colorado border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 5-10 inches were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month of well below average precipitation kept all of Southwest and South-Central New Mexico in Extreme(D3) to Exceptional(D4) drought. Most locations saw less than a half inch of rain during the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme(D3) drought continued through the month of June across the area. The Cooperative Observer at Faywood reported 0.01 inches of precipitation which was 1.04 inches below normal.

An area of virga showers and dry thunderstorms over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains produced strong winds at the Taos Municipal Airport.  A peak wind gust of 78mph was recorded.  No reports of damage were received however one large plane was spun around.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind spun a 9000 pound Aero Commander 690 twin engine turbo prop about 120 degrees.  Aircraft undamaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed across northern New Mexico producing locally heavy rainfall, gusty winds, and small hail. One brief strong thunderstorm developed around the Taos area and produced penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny hail reported 6 miles south of Taos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level short wave trough combined with sufficient low level moisture and strong instability to produce isolated severe thunderstorms stretching from De Baca County northeast to Union County.  Reports of half dollar to ping pong ball size hail were received around the Yeso area with damage to trees.  Heavy rainfall was also reported with these storms.  No reports were found of severe weather in southern Union county however radar indicated very strong signatures for severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail near Yeso.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity drastically increased across portions of central and western New Mexico throughout the month of April. ||Only a few storms brought precipitation to the state during the month, and those storms were focused on northern areas of the state.  Percent of normal precipitation was well below normal for northern and central New Mexico. Most locations in eastern and central New Mexico were under 25% of normal. Along the Rio Grande Valley percent of normal inched up to 25-50% of normal. Areas in the west, northwest and north central ranged from 50-90% of normal with some isolated pockets of values at or over 100% mostly in the higher terrain areas along the peaks of mountain ranges.||To start the month off Extreme drought (D3) covered nearly 50% of the state covering most of the eastern plains extending as far west as the west central mountains with some Exceptional drought (D4) speckled across this area. Severe drought (D2) covered the remainder of the area with some Moderate drought (D1) holding on in the San Francisco River Valley and in portions of the far northwest highlands. One week into the month D3 conditions had crept all the way to the western border between AZ and NM in the west central plateau.  Into the latter half of the month D3 conditions had increased to cover 77% of the area with all areas in the northwest returning to D2. By the end of the month with most of the state in D3, D4 conditions increased along central NM in the Rio Grande Valley extending from Bernalillo past Socorro southward. D4 increased from 4% at the beginning of the month to 24% by months end. The far northwest highland zone which was making an attempt at getting out of drought was sucked back into D2 conditions. The San Francisco River Valley zone which was had thus far managed to stay out of drought conditions was no longer successfully and now every zone in the ABQ CWA is experiencing  D2 or higher drought conditions.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system centered off the California coast spread precipitation over portions of New Mexico throughout the day on the 8th.  Initial rain bands formed in central and western New Mexico and brought decent amounts of precipitation along with thunder and lightning. Snow began to fall in western and northern high terrain areas of the state into the evening of the 8th.  As the low moved east throughout the overnight hours, by around 11am, the low center was near the Four Corners region. The low began to elongate into the afternoon of the 9th as it ejected out of northeastern Colorado with continued wrap around precipitation affecting northeastern New Mexico through early Sunday morning. This system brought moderate snow to areas of New Mexico along with high winds which caused near white out conditions along the New Mexico/Colorado border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 6-22 inches were reported.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tres Lagunas Fire burned 10,219 acres within and near prized recreation and resource areas. The fire was human-caused when electrical lines went down on May 30, igniting Ponderosa pine and other timbers. The assessment report by the Burned Area Emergency Response team indicated this was a relatively healthy wildfire burn and included a small area of re-burn in the 2002 Viveash Fire. Approximately 15% of the burn was high intensity, 13% moderate, and 72% low. The National Interagency Coordination Center indicated the total cost of this fire will near $3.5 million, including efforts to reduce the effects of monsoonal flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed power lines ignited a wildfire along highway 63 near the vacation community of Tres Lagunas. The wildfire spread from 7,395 acres at the end of May to just under 10,300 acres by June 12th.  The fire did not experience any further growth through early July before being declared 100% contained on July 5th.  Extensive efforts have been put forth to mitigate the impacts from monsoon flooding.

A powerful upper level low pressure system that intensified over Colorado forced a potent dry slot and associated strong winds over New Mexico.  Many locations across central and eastern New Mexico reported peak wind gusts up to 60 mph, with a few locations across the eastern plains peaking up to 70 mph. Numerous reports of damage were received across the region.  Widespread blowing dust across the region reduced visibilities below 3 miles, with a few areas of Curry and Roosevelt counties down below one quarter mile for several hours.  Intense crosswinds along a section of railroad near Fort Sumner overturned 30 to 40 cars of a BNSF train carrying cases of tuna.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half of sheet metal roof blown off business at 4th Street and Main Street in downtown Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong back door cold front plunged down the eastern plains throughout the day on May 1. Very gusty winds developed behind this feature. Significant wind speeds were focused in the northeast and eastern portions of the state throughout the day with the front stalling out along the east central plains in the afternoon. Close to sunset the front began its second surge south and west across the state. During the evening hours into the early morning hours many location in the upper Tularosa valley and the south central mountains hit high wind criteria. In the areas where the highest wind was recorded forecast 3-hour pressure changes were 17-20 mb, and the locations pin pointed by these changes are where the highest winds occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple Mesonet sites from this zone hit wind gusts ranging from 50mph-65mph during the early morning hours of the second.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity decreased across many areas of western and central New Mexico courtesy of a robust start to the 2013 monsoon season.  Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees snapped and uprooted at Indian School and University.

Significant rain fell across most of Southwest and South-Central New Mexico in the month of July as the monsoon season went into full effect. Many parts of Southwest New Mexico into the Gila region received over an inch more rain than average for the month. All areas were brought down to Extreme(D3) or better drought conditions after most areas were in Exceptional(D4) drought earlier in the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone transitioned from Exceptional(D4) to Extreme(D3) drought on July 30th. The Cloudcroft Cooperative Observer reported 6.50 inches of rain which was 0.51 inches above normal.

All areas were brought down to Extreme(D3) or better drought conditions after most areas were in Exceptional(D4) drought earlier in the year.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity decreased across many areas of western and central New Mexico courtesy of a robust start to the 2013 monsoon season.  Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

A broad ridge of high pressure aloft stretched from west Texas into much of New Mexico and resulted in weak steering flow. An isolated strong thunderstorm developed over the Little Bear burn scar near Ruidoso at midday and then into a line with embedded storms from north to south over the area. The storm remained over the burn scar for nearly an hour and a half with light rain for an hour or two thereafter. A road within the burn scar was washed out due to the continuous heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager relayed that Bonito Lake Rd. near Bonito Lake was washed out. Storm cell was stationary over area for nearly one and a half hours before it dissipated. Rain gauges across the burn scar area reported rainfall amounts ranging from 0.70-1.50 during this time. A car was washed off the road in flooding location with one occupant needing rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager as well as local media reported that the entire city of Corrales was flooded due to heavy rains. Several businesses were flooded as well as numerous cars and neighborhoods. Many roads were also washed out. Trees were downed due to the wind and many traffic lights were out. Rainfall reports of up to nearly 3 inches of rain were reported in association with this storm. Residents were still cleaning up the damage throughout the following weekend.

High pressure aloft centered over Nevada maintained a persistent northerly flow over New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the peaks of the northern high terrain and drifted south throughout the day as storms increased in both coverage and intensity. Some of the storms became severe as vertical wind shear was more than sufficient to support organized storms. The main threat was gusty outflow winds, especially for western New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high centered over New Mexico continued to dominate the weather pattern. Ample moisture in place provided for a 'recycle' mode pattern. Slow-moving storms with significant moisture caused flooding in areas of central New Mexico throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain and drifted slowly east onto the adjacent plains and highlands. Storms continued to develop into the late evening hours in the Albuquerque South Valley prompting evacuations in some areas due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes were damaged by flood waters in the Albuquerque South Valley. The heavy rain began during the evening hours around 830pm and continued until close to 1030pm with rainfall amounts ranging from 0.50 to nearly 2.70.  The Albuquerque Metro Area Flood Control Authority reported that Amole Dam retained an estimated 400 acre-feet of storm-water, which was the first time the dam had nearly filled since it was built in the early 1990s. The Amole Dam is 2000 feet long, 700 feet wide, and 33 feet deep.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a major mudslide/debris flow near Bobcat Pass on NM 38 north of Eagle Nest. Several vehicles were directly impacted as rocks and sediment were washed onto the road blocking both lanes of traffic. A snow plow was used to clear debris and the road was reopened about an hour later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clayton Police Dept. relayed reports of a funnel cloud associated with a severe thunderstorm storm in the area.  A large funnel cloud was accompanied by several smaller funnel clouds. Rotation was not observed or evident on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was parked squarely over New Mexico. Overnight showers across the Gila region in southwestern NM were responsible for significant rainfall amounts across the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar. This caused some flooding with debris deposits along roads in the area. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain then drifted over the plains and adjacent highlands. Storm motions were generally very slow to the north or northeast. Flooding developed at Los Alamos National Labs as an isolated thunderstorm formed directly overhead and remained nearly stationary for an extended period of time. There was also a lightning injury reported from another isolated storm near Santa Fe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Morning rainfall that ranged from 1 to nearly 3 in some areas around the burn scar caused heavy flows in creeks in the area which in turn deposited debris on roads. Roads were closed until the Department of Transportation could clear them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface boundary was located through Southwest New Mexico with a weak upper disturbance moving around southwestern periphery of upper high. Isolated late evening thunderstorms developed and brought golf ball size hail and severe wind gusts to the Virden area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported golf ball size hail and damage to crops and trees. There was also a broken house window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northerly flow continued across the region as an upper low retrograded to near Kansas City, MO and upper ridge remained over Great Basin. A strong 85+ kt jet and weak upper disturbance was moving through Eastern Colorado and into Northern New Mexico aiding in lift for thunderstorm development. Up to golf ball size hail was reported along with a couple areas of flash flooding due to training of storms over mountain areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported on Highway 244 east of Cloudcroft which broke a windshield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture was recycled under the upper level high throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms began to develop in the northeast and the southwest during the early morning hours before new development commenced in the northern high terrain. With the upper level high shifting towards the west,  storm motions across the northern half of the state were to the south while storms in the south were to the north, both under 10 mph. Coverage increased into the evening hours across the north and east becoming scattered to numerous. A large swath of precipitation moved down eastern New Mexico with thunderstorm cells also developing around the Albuquerque area causing flooding from heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Double Eagle ASOS on Albuquerque's west side reported a high wind gust. A truck and trailer were blown over on Interstate 40 near this site during this time frame.

An upper level disturbance pushed southeast across northern New Mexico and interacted with a stationary frontal boundary in the Rio Grande Valley.  The combination of abundant moisture and instability and northwest flow aloft produced repeated rounds of heavy rainfall across areas in northern as well as central portions of the state including the Las Conchas burn scar and the Albuquerque Metro Area. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were reported with these storms. Flooding initially occurred in north-central NM during the afternoon and evening hours before training cells developed in northern Bernalillo County during the overnight hours. The Interstate 25 corridor around Bernalillo into north Albuquerque was one of the hardest hit. Widespread flooding was reported in Bernalillo due to the heavy rainfall and a breached acequia. Up to 5 feet of water was reported in one home in Bernalillo.  Frequent cloud to ground lightning strikes created numerous power outages across Santa Fe with nearly 30,000 customers out in Albuquerque. Lightning was reported to have hit Sandia Casino in northeast Albuquerque shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home flooded in Bernalillo and 3 to 4 homes flooded in El Llanito.  Mud and debris from the flood waters significantly impacted the area and clean up cost thousands of dollars beyond damage to structures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A microburst from a thunderstorm that moved over Socorro produced isolated strong wind gusts up to an estimated 70mph.  The metal roof was torn off the Socorro County annex building on Neel Street.  The burglar alarm activated as the roof tore off alerting emergency management staff to the damaged structure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The metal roof of the Socorro County annex building was partially peeled off and significantly damaged.
