Upper level high pressure centered over the Great Basin continued a north to south flow aloft over New Mexico. Sufficient moisture in place across the area fueled thunderstorm development along the eastern periphery of the upper high. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain before moving south and then southwest. Isolated strong to severe storms developed in the afternoon with numerous reports of large hail and severe winds and minor road flooding along with copious amounts of mainly small hail across the eastern plains.  Among the most severe storms struck Santa Rosa, New Mexico.  Nickel to golfball-sized hail fell in the Santa Rosa area resulting in major damage. The smaller hail accumulated up two feet deep in some spots and snow plows were used to clear roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeply saturated atmosphere and weak steering flow over New Mexico set the stage for slow-moving heavy thunderstorms with torrential rainfall.  Rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour in some areas.  The greatest impact from heavy rainfall occurred in the area from near Santa Fe south through Eldorado and Lamy to Cerrillos, Madrid, and Albuquerque where significant flash flooding developed.  Strong winds and large hail accompanied the heavy rainfall in the Albuquerque Metro Area.  Another area of heavy rainfall produced flash flooding around El Rito and Ojo Caliente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of man hole covers were blown out of roadway from flash flooding around Spain and Eubank.  Widespread flooded roadways were reported within this area.  Small sink hole developed at the intersection of Copper and Juan Tabo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent area of torrential rainfall over the headwaters of Santa Clara Canyon produced a significant flash flood wave on Santa Clara Creek. Rainfall rates with this storm approached 6 inches per hour leading to storm total amounts near 3 inches. A 9 foot wave of water surged through Santa Clara Canyon wiping out highways 601 and 602.  The flood wave moved downstream and impacted the NM 30 bridge at Santa Clara Pueblo. Flash Flooding was also reported in eastern Bernalillo County as a strong, slow-moving storm dropped heavy rain just south of Tijeras for close to an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall rate of nearly 6 inches per hour over the Deer Pond gage produced 2.74 inches of rain in less than 30 minutes.  This heavy rainfall produced a nearly 10 foot flood wave on Santa Clara Creek.

Frequent lightning strikes and strong gusty winds were also reported with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust to 59mph reported 2 miles east of Stanley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local COOP observer estimated a wind gust to near 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture was recycled under the upper level high throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms began to develop in the northeast and the southwest during the early morning hours before new development commenced in the northern high terrain. With the upper level high shifting towards the west,  storm motions across the northern half of the state were to the south while storms in the south were to the north, both under 10 mph. Coverage increased into the evening hours across the north and east becoming scattered to numerous. A large swath of precipitation moved down eastern New Mexico with thunderstorm cells also developing around the Albuquerque area causing flooding from heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in association with KRTN Radio in Raton reported and showed photos of flooding near Maxwell, NM. Over 3 was reported to have fallen in parts of town with drainages running full. Irrigation ponds north of town were also filled with up to 12 of standing water in nearby agricultural fields. No flooding issues were reported on major roadways, but local unpaved roads were turned into creeks along with the fields as water collected in low spots. An initial isolated cell developed over the location around 12:30pm and after that training cells went over the location for the next couple hours. By 5:00pm, flooding had occurred throughout the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity decreased across many areas of western and central New Mexico courtesy of a robust start to the 2013 monsoon season.  Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Patchy to severe blowing dust kicked up along U.S. 70 along the Curry County / Roosevelt County line just south of Clovis. Visibility was estimated at 1/4 mile. An outflow boundary from activity in Texas moved into the affected area and continued to move southwest. A single storm cell developed in the area as well as this boundary passed through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level low pressure system moved westward into portions of eastern New Mexico. Substantial atmospheric moisture paired with the destabilization from the incoming low produced slow-moving showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall. During the early afternoon,  storms developed on the northern periphery of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar in Catron County causing flooding in Hail Canyon along Forest Road 141.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred in Hail Canyon along Forest Road 141 on the northern periphery of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar. Rainfall amounts ranging from 0.25 - 0.75 were reported across the entirety of the burn scar. Pictures showed significant amounts of large rocks and sediment were deposited on this road. Crews had to plow road before it was passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of roads flooded, washed out, and damaged in eastern portion of Socorro County.  Emergency Manager indicated cost of damage was near 1.2 million dollars.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeply saturated atmosphere along with a well-defined stationary frontal boundary over northeastern New Mexico served as a focus for the development of severe thunderstorms and flash flooding.  Activity most significantly impacted Union and Harding counties.  Heavy rainfall and severe weather continued to spread farther south across Quay, Curry, and Roosevelt counties where radar estimated between 3 and 6 inches of rain in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry Cimarron Creek at Folsom became a raging torrent of water with flows out of bank into nearby trees and meadows.  Photos from one section of the creek showed a large waterfall scouring out the creek bed and nearby creek banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high centered over New Mexico continued to dominate the weather pattern. Ample moisture in place provided for a 'recycle' mode pattern. Slow-moving storms with significant moisture caused flooding in areas of central New Mexico throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain and drifted slowly east onto the adjacent plains and highlands. Storms continued to develop into the late evening hours in the Albuquerque South Valley prompting evacuations in some areas due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on Albuquerque's West-side led to street flooding with some vehicles partially submerged on the side of roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The body of an adult female was recovered from an arroyo on the south side of Santa Fe during the late evening hours. Additionally, one to two feet of water was reported on some roads on the south side of Santa Fe.  Rainfall reports ranged from 0.50-1.41. Dry arroyos and streams in the area ran deep and fast well into the evening as a result of runoff from the storm. Arroyos were reported to be filled with about 8 feet of fast-moving water at the peak of the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was parked squarely over New Mexico. Overnight showers across the Gila region in southwestern NM were responsible for significant rainfall amounts across the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar. This caused some flooding with debris deposits along roads in the area. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain then drifted over the plains and adjacent highlands. Storm motions were generally very slow to the north or northeast. Flooding developed at Los Alamos National Labs as an isolated thunderstorm formed directly overhead and remained nearly stationary for an extended period of time. There was also a lightning injury reported from another isolated storm near Santa Fe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos of a small funnel cloud over the Grants area were received from the public.

A deeply saturated atmosphere and weak steering flow over New Mexico set the stage for slow-moving heavy thunderstorms with torrential rainfall.  Rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour in some areas.  The greatest impact from heavy rainfall occurred in the area from near Santa Fe south through Eldorado and Lamy to Cerrillos, Madrid, and Albuquerque where significant flash flooding developed.  Strong winds and large hail accompanied the heavy rainfall in the Albuquerque Metro Area.  Another area of heavy rainfall produced flash flooding around El Rito and Ojo Caliente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gold Mine Road was flooded and impassable for nearly 200 yards near Cerrillos. Major torrents of water raging in San Marcos Arroyo between Eldorado and NM 14 near Cerrillos were captured by local residents, many of whom described it as a rare sight.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A major mudslide off the hill behind property ended up a couple feet deep on the deck and patio. Unpaved driveway completely destroyed by flood waters. The arroyo that crossed the property along NM 14 was at least 40 feet wide and 10 feet deep.

A persistent area of torrential rainfall over the headwaters of Santa Clara Canyon produced a significant flash flood wave on Santa Clara Creek. Rainfall rates with this storm approached 6 inches per hour leading to storm total amounts near 3 inches. A 9 foot wave of water surged through Santa Clara Canyon wiping out highways 601 and 602.  The flood wave moved downstream and impacted the NM 30 bridge at Santa Clara Pueblo. Flash Flooding was also reported in eastern Bernalillo County as a strong, slow-moving storm dropped heavy rain just south of Tijeras for close to an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was observed over several roadways in Santa Clara Canyon including 601 and 602. Flash flood wave made it to the NM 30 bridge with no damage apparent.  However, significant damage occurred to several reservation roads, culverts, diversion structures, and trees through the canyon.  A storage building was engulfed in several feet of mud.  The Santa Clara Pueblo received a Presidential Disaster Declaration for this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of slow-moving thunderstorms pushed southeast across the Thompson Ridge burn scar during the early to mid afternoon hours.  Heavy rainfall overwhelmed several creeks in the area producing flash flooding along the western periphery of the burn scar.  Flash flooding was observed along Forest Service Road 105 where culverts were destroyed due to mud and debris. Elk Valley Estates was also impacted by a significant torrent of water, mud, and debris however no structures were lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Culvert on forest service 105 destroyed due to fast flowing water, mud, and debris.  60ft wide torrent of water, mud, and debris also impacted Elk Valley Estates however no structures were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon burst pattern developed across central and southern New Mexico as deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over the area.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas of Catron, Socorro, Lincoln, and Torrance counties.  Several reports of flooded roadways were received across these areas.  Frequent lightning strikes and strong gusty winds were also reported with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: WSMR site Phillips Hill reported peak wind gust to 77mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A monsoon burst pattern developed across central and southern New Mexico as deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over the area.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas of Catron, Socorro, Lincoln, and Torrance counties.  Several reports of flooded roadways were received across these areas.  Frequent lightning strikes and strong gusty winds were also reported with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car traveling east bound on Interstate 40 three miles east of Moriarty at mile marker 199 with 4 occupants was struck by lightning. One child and one adult were injured and treated at the scene. Car's electronics were destroyed and vehicle towed from scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several vehicles submerged on Central Avenue under the railroad tracks. AMAFCA noted that at one point the Broadway Pump Station did not activate. This may have been due to a power failure. This caused some flooding at Broadway and Lomas. That water flowed south and taxed the storm drainage network to the south. The South Broadway Detention Basin may have overtopped. At Tingley Park (which is a surge pond) the ring and cover was knocked loose by the water in the storm drain but it is unclear whether or not the Alcalde and Barelas Pump Stations then failed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity decreased across many areas of western and central New Mexico courtesy of a robust start to the 2013 monsoon season.  Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

High pressure aloft centered over Nevada maintained a persistent northerly flow over New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the peaks of the northern high terrain and drifted south throughout the day as storms increased in both coverage and intensity. Some of the storms became severe as vertical wind shear was more than sufficient to support organized storms. The main threat was gusty outflow winds, especially for western New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County Emergency Manager relayed a report from the Capitan Police Dept. Chief of a well-defined funnel cloud just north of Capitan.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail resulted in damage to automobiles, including broken windshields.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture was recycled under the upper level high throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms began to develop in the northeast and the southwest during the early morning hours before new development commenced in the northern high terrain. With the upper level high shifting towards the west,  storm motions across the northern half of the state were to the south while storms in the south were to the north, both under 10 mph. Coverage increased into the evening hours across the north and east becoming scattered to numerous. A large swath of precipitation moved down eastern New Mexico with thunderstorm cells also developing around the Albuquerque area causing flooding from heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Embudo Canyon weather station near Indian School and Tramway recorded gusts from 55-61 mph during the evening hours. Storms were in the vicinity of an outflow boundary that passed across the location 30 to 45 minutes before this gust was recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deeply saturated atmosphere and weak steering flow over New Mexico set the stage for slow-moving heavy thunderstorms with torrential rainfall.  Rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour in some areas.  The greatest impact from heavy rainfall occurred in the area from near Santa Fe south through Eldorado and Lamy to Cerrillos, Madrid, and Albuquerque where significant flash flooding developed.  Strong winds and large hail accompanied the heavy rainfall in the Albuquerque Metro Area.  Another area of heavy rainfall produced flash flooding around El Rito and Ojo Caliente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter hail at Montgomery and Moon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high pressure system was centered over northern New Mexico. Deep moisture along with light easterly steering flow led to locally heavy rainfall in association with isolated strong storms. The central mountain chain was favored for thunderstorm initiation through the late morning and early afternoon hours before the storms drifted onto the adjacent plains and highlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Fe New Mexican reported that Pecos Canyon was hit hard by flash flooding. The flooding apparently occurred in the canyon above Brush Ranch River Lodge on NM 63. Large boulders along with sludge and debris were carried down the canyon. The flooding apparently came down three different ravines that are in the area, over topped sandbags and left about 4-5 feet of muddy water on the property. The Tres Lagunas burn scar is also in this area and the debris that affected this property came from the burnt trees and sludge from the fire. Crews worked the following day to remove debris with heavy equipment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was parked squarely over New Mexico. Overnight showers across the Gila region in southwestern NM were responsible for significant rainfall amounts across the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar. This caused some flooding with debris deposits along roads in the area. Afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain then drifted over the plains and adjacent highlands. Storm motions were generally very slow to the north or northeast. Flooding developed at Los Alamos National Labs as an isolated thunderstorm formed directly overhead and remained nearly stationary for an extended period of time. There was also a lightning injury reported from another isolated storm near Santa Fe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Fe New Mexican ran story about lightning hitting a large cottonwood tree in the Santa Fe Plaza. The bolt hit the tree and sent branches flying into a nearby crowd of people that were gathered in downtown for weekend festivities. Newspaper reporting that at least one man was injured from flying debris while another had breathing problems for a short time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant rain fell across most of Southwest and South-Central New Mexico in the month of July as the monsoon season went into full effect. Many parts of Southwest New Mexico into the Gila region received over an inch more rain than average for the month. All areas were brought down to Extreme(D3) or better drought conditions after most areas were in Exceptional(D4) drought earlier in the year.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone transitioned from Extreme(D3) drought to Severe(D2) drought on July 23rd. Gila Hot Springs reported 3.75 inches of rain which was 1.10 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure centered over the Great Basin continued a north to south flow aloft over New Mexico. Sufficient moisture in place across the area fueled thunderstorm development along the eastern periphery of the upper high. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain before moving south and then southwest. Isolated strong to severe storms developed in the afternoon with numerous reports of large hail and severe winds and minor road flooding along with copious amounts of mainly small hail across the eastern plains.  Among the most severe storms struck Santa Rosa, New Mexico.  Nickel to golfball-sized hail fell in the Santa Rosa area resulting in major damage. The smaller hail accumulated up two feet deep in some spots and snow plows were used to clear roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity decreased across many areas of western and central New Mexico courtesy of a robust start to the 2013 monsoon season.  Near to well above normal precipitation was observed nearly statewide for the month. The first seven days of July were especially active with 7-day observed rainfall totals exceeding two inches at many locations from southwest to northeast New Mexico. It was the 2nd wettest first seven days of July at the Albuquerque Foothills and Clines Corners since 1992, when official record keeping began.  Most central locales received 200-400 percent of normal precipitation for the month.  Areas across the east were generally 75 to 100 percent of normal precipitation, while parts of Guadalupe, De Baca and Chaves counties experienced rainfall totals as much as four times greater than average. The far west and southwestern locales experienced precipitation totals that were 75 to 125 percent of average.  ||The most extreme drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), was significantly reduced to approximately one-quarter of the state from nearly half in June 2013.  The second highest drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), also decreased in areal coverage from 92% at the start of the month to 74% by month's end. The remaining categories were largely unchanged with only a slight decrease in overall coverage.

A deeply saturated atmosphere and weak steering flow over New Mexico set the stage for slow-moving heavy thunderstorms with torrential rainfall.  Rainfall rates approached 2 inches per hour in some areas.  The greatest impact from heavy rainfall occurred in the area from near Santa Fe south through Eldorado and Lamy to Cerrillos, Madrid, and Albuquerque where significant flash flooding developed.  Strong winds and large hail accompanied the heavy rainfall in the Albuquerque Metro Area.  Another area of heavy rainfall produced flash flooding around El Rito and Ojo Caliente.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 111 washed away along Rio Vallecitos. Vehicles parked at Ojo Caliente Mineral Springs Resort & Spa were among the damages and mudslides were reported along the hillsides near the resort. The RV and camping area was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure centered over the Great Basin continued a north to south flow aloft over New Mexico. Sufficient moisture in place across the area fueled thunderstorm development along the eastern periphery of the upper high. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain before moving south and then southwest. Isolated strong to severe storms developed in the afternoon with numerous reports of large hail and severe winds and minor road flooding along with copious amounts of mainly small hail across the eastern plains.  Among the most severe storms struck Santa Rosa, New Mexico.  Nickel to golfball-sized hail fell in the Santa Rosa area resulting in major damage. The smaller hail accumulated up two feet deep in some spots and snow plows were used to clear roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees down in the area around Randolph and Yale Rds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was located across Southwest New Mexico with a weak mid level trough off to the southwest. A deep layer of moisture was in place across the region with a wet microburst reported in Columbus, NM with winds of up to 90 mph reported. In addition quarter sized hail was reported around Hurley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee driving through Columbus reported damage to several manufactured homes. Based on photos and wind damage it was determined to be a microburst with winds of up to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high centered over New Mexico continued to dominate the weather pattern. Ample moisture in place provided for a 'recycle' mode pattern. Slow-moving storms with significant moisture caused flooding in areas of central New Mexico throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain and drifted slowly east onto the adjacent plains and highlands. Storms continued to develop into the late evening hours in the Albuquerque South Valley prompting evacuations in some areas due to flooding.

A monsoon burst pattern developed across central and southern New Mexico as deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance over the area.  Slow-moving thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall across many areas of Catron, Socorro, Lincoln, and Torrance counties.  Several reports of flooded roadways were received across these areas.  Frequent lightning strikes and strong gusty winds were also reported with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was destroyed by a lightning strikes along highway 47.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated complex of gusty showers developed into a line of thunderstorms over east central New Mexico by late afternoon.  This line of thunderstorms accelerated quickly while producing strong winds up to 60 mph and very little rainfall.  A significant dust storm occurred along the gust front of these thunderstorms with several residents reporting visibilities down to zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds from collapsing thunderstorms several miles west of Clayton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front brought an increase in moisture as it made its way south and westward across eastern portions of New Mexico.  An upper level shortwave trough was also present which further increased the chances for storm activity. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours with numerous severe reports.  Large hail and severe winds were reported across much of the area from Quay County south through Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Power lines were downed across several areas of Roosevelt County.  Accumulations of smaller hail along Interstate 25 at La Bajada Hill south of Santa Fe resulted in a jack-knifed semi-trailer and a roll over crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight Mile Draw RAWS recorded this gust in Chaves County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich airmass was in place along and east of the central mountain chain. With the increased moisture and adequate instability some isolated severe storms developed in southern Colorado and migrated to the southeast into northeast New Mexico. Some storms also developed over the the Sangre de Cristo mountains and these migrated to the east into the northeast highlands throughout the afternoon into evening hours. A series of long track severe storms in particular impacted a narrow region with severe hail from near Raton southeastward across the plains to south of Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter hail in Sedan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity continued to increase across portions of northern and western New Mexico while decreasing ever so slightly  in the east. ||Above to well-above normal precipitation was observed  over parts of central and much of eastern New Mexico due in large part to  several severe weather outbreaks. Much of the central portion of the state  received between 50 and 100 percent of normal precipitation, while areas across the east were generally above above normal including a few areas that experienced up to 200 percent of normal precipitation. The most significant rainfall was noted in Roosevelt County along with portions of Chaves and De Baca counties where up to three times the normal monthly precipitation fell. Meanwhile, the northwest and much of the far west experienced very little thunderstorm activity-- typical of June.||June 2013 had very little change in the overall areal coverage of severe or worse drought conditions. The worst drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), continued to account for nearly half of the state at 43 percent, encompassing most of central New Mexico as well as the eastern half of the state. The second worst drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), increased to 92 percent of the state from 84 percent observed at the end of May. This was mainly due to the lack of significant precipitation across the northwest plateau and highland areas in western and northwestern New Mexico. Severe drought coverage remained largely unchanged.

A deep surge of monsoonal moisture allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to impact New Mexico on the afternoon of the 1st.  Heavy rainfall over the Whitewater- Baldy burn scar in Catron County forced flood debris onto Bursum Road over a several mile stretch east of Mogollon.  Another storm around Edgewood washed out a portion of a roadway and caused damage to a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bursum Road closed from near Mogollon east about 10 miles due to debris and flooding over the roadway.

This lift and moisture combined with good instability to produce hail, strong winds, and flash flooding across New Mexico and West Texas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dryline across the eastern plains of New Mexico provided a focus for several severe thunderstorms by late afternoon.  Surface dewpoints to the east of the dryline were in the lower to middle 60s while dewpoints in the single digits were pushing in from the west.  The main focus for severe weather occurred along the dryline from near Tucumcari south to around Fort Sumner and east of the Roswell area.  Numerous reports of large hail, strong gusty winds, and hazardous blowing dust were reported, along with a funnel cloud a few miles north of Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny hail at Sumner Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated complex of gusty showers developed into a line of thunderstorms over east central New Mexico by late afternoon.  This line of thunderstorms accelerated quickly while producing strong winds up to 60 mph and very little rainfall.  A significant dust storm occurred along the gust front of these thunderstorms with several residents reporting visibilities down to zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow winds along thunderstorm gust front near Ragland with visibility reduced to zero in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined upper level shortwave diving south out of the central Rockies interacted with deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Severe outflow winds, localized wind damage, large hail, locally heavy rainfall, and significant blowing dust were reported with some of these storms. The most severe thunderstorms produced baseball size hail around Arabela and damaging wind in excess of 70mph around Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White Rock Los Alamos National Lab array 54.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity continued to increase across portions of northern and western New Mexico while decreasing ever so slightly  in the east. ||Above to well-above normal precipitation was observed  over parts of central and much of eastern New Mexico due in large part to  several severe weather outbreaks. Much of the central portion of the state  received between 50 and 100 percent of normal precipitation, while areas across the east were generally above above normal including a few areas that experienced up to 200 percent of normal precipitation. The most significant rainfall was noted in Roosevelt County along with portions of Chaves and De Baca counties where up to three times the normal monthly precipitation fell. Meanwhile, the northwest and much of the far west experienced very little thunderstorm activity-- typical of June.||June 2013 had very little change in the overall areal coverage of severe or worse drought conditions. The worst drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), continued to account for nearly half of the state at 43 percent, encompassing most of central New Mexico as well as the eastern half of the state. The second worst drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), increased to 92 percent of the state from 84 percent observed at the end of May. This was mainly due to the lack of significant precipitation across the northwest plateau and highland areas in western and northwestern New Mexico. Severe drought coverage remained largely unchanged.

Thunderstorms developed along the east slopes of the central mountain chain and pushed slowly south southeast across the high plains.  Most of the activity produced only light to moderate rainfall amounts and gusty winds.  However, a strong storm did manage to develop and produce nickel size hail along Highway 285 in extreme southwestern De Baca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported from off duty NWS employee on Highway 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined upper level shortwave diving south out of the central Rockies interacted with deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Severe outflow winds, localized wind damage, large hail, locally heavy rainfall, and significant blowing dust were reported with some of these storms. The most severe thunderstorms produced baseball size hail around Arabela and damaging wind in excess of 70mph around Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter hail on Interstate 40 between milemarker 238 and 239.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front brought an increase in moisture as it made its way south and westward across eastern portions of New Mexico.  An upper level shortwave trough was also present which further increased the chances for storm activity. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours with numerous severe reports.  Large hail and severe winds were reported across much of the area from Quay County south through Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Power lines were downed across several areas of Roosevelt County.  Accumulations of smaller hail along Interstate 25 at La Bajada Hill south of Santa Fe resulted in a jack-knifed semi-trailer and a roll over crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail lasted for about 45 minutes and the largest stone was the size of a quarter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fast northwest flow aloft with remnant moisture in place over the east central plains produced an isolated severe thunderstorm in Curry County.  Quarter size hail was reported for several minutes near Grady.  Several wall clouds were also reported with this storm as it pushed south across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter hail 2 miles southwest of Grady.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline in place over eastern New Mexico provided a focus for afternoon thunderstorms over the east central and southeast plains.  The main impact of these storms were damaging winds. A severe wind gust was reported near Dora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dora Mesonet recorded a gust to 58mph as well as a high sustained wind speed of 42mph a a line of strong thunderstorms was moving through the area.

This was mainly due to the lack of significant precipitation across the northwest plateau and highland areas in western and northwestern New Mexico. Severe drought coverage remained largely unchanged.

A backdoor cold front brought an increase in moisture as it made its way south and westward across eastern portions of New Mexico.  An upper level shortwave trough was also present which further increased the chances for storm activity. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours with numerous severe reports.  Large hail and severe winds were reported across much of the area from Quay County south through Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Power lines were downed across several areas of Roosevelt County.  Accumulations of smaller hail along Interstate 25 at La Bajada Hill south of Santa Fe resulted in a jack-knifed semi-trailer and a roll over crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recorded by the CVN ASOS in Curry County in association with thunderstorm activity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline in place over eastern New Mexico provided a focus for afternoon thunderstorms over the east central and southeast plains.  The main impact of these storms were damaging winds. A severe wind gust was reported near Dora.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage survey was a classic dry microburst event.  Unfortunately,  there were no direct measurements of wind speeds but the toolkit conservatively estimated wind speeds of 60-70 mph, based on the type of power pole,  orientation, etc. The winds were from the north-northwest and may have been accelerated by the terrain along the immediate west slopes of the Sandias. The damage area was extremely small at 1/2km or less bounded by Tramway on the east,  Montgomery on the north,  Comanche on the south,  and extended maybe 1/4 mile west of Tramway.

Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed along the east slopes of the central mountain chain as a back door cold front made its way southward. Thunderstorms initially developed over the higher terrain of central New Mexico then moved east across the northeast highlands as well as the eastern plains. Multiple reports of mainly pea to dime size hail were received along with heavy downpours, but damaging straight line winds were the primary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny hail reported 10 miles east southeast of Tucumcari.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm developed over Quay County around 5pm and slowly moved southeast impacting portions of Curry County during the evening.  This storm produced strong, gusty winds and hailstones as large as ping pong ball size.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Marble to ping pong ball size hail for about 10 minutes 10 miles south of Grady.

Moisture increased from south to north through the day on June 2, 2013. With adequate instability in place showers and thunderstorms first began in western areas along the Continental Divide. As the day progressed the storms increased in coverage and intensity while propagating eastward. The storms continued on an easterly track with some redevelopment in the west during the evening hours. This redevelopment also moved east throughout the evening.  These storms produced mainly erratic strong wind gusts and an isolated hail report near Bingham.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter in Stanley reported a peak wind gust to 58mph.

An upper level shortwave trough was also present which further increased the chances for storm activity. Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours with numerous severe reports.  Large hail and severe winds were reported across much of the area from Quay County south through Curry and Roosevelt counties.  Power lines were downed across several areas of Roosevelt County.  Accumulations of smaller hail along Interstate 25 at La Bajada Hill south of Santa Fe resulted in a jack-knifed semi-trailer and a roll over crash.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported near Pendaries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought severity continued to increase across portions of northern and western New Mexico while decreasing ever so slightly  in the east. ||Above to well-above normal precipitation was observed  over parts of central and much of eastern New Mexico due in large part to  several severe weather outbreaks. Much of the central portion of the state  received between 50 and 100 percent of normal precipitation, while areas across the east were generally above above normal including a few areas that experienced up to 200 percent of normal precipitation. The most significant rainfall was noted in Roosevelt County along with portions of Chaves and De Baca counties where up to three times the normal monthly precipitation fell. Meanwhile, the northwest and much of the far west experienced very little thunderstorm activity-- typical of June.||June 2013 had very little change in the overall areal coverage of severe or worse drought conditions. The worst drought category, Exceptional Drought (D4), continued to account for nearly half of the state at 43 percent, encompassing most of central New Mexico as well as the eastern half of the state. The second worst drought category, Extreme Drought (D3), increased to 92 percent of the state from 84 percent observed at the end of May. This was mainly due to the lack of significant precipitation across the northwest plateau and highland areas in western and northwestern New Mexico. Severe drought coverage remained largely unchanged.

A seven month old boy was in critical condition after being left inside a hot car for more than two hours during the afternoon of Monday, June 10th.  Temperatures around the city at the time of the incident were in the upper 90s to low 100s. The ASOS at the Albuquerque Sunport recorded a maximum temperature of 99 degrees Fahrenheit, which tied the record maximum temperature for the date last observed in 1981.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An infant left inside a hot car for over 2 hours during the afternoon was left in critical condition due to the heat. Temperatures around the city were in the upper 90's to low 100's.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep surge of monsoonal moisture allowed for numerous showers and thunderstorms to impact New Mexico on the afternoon of the 1st.  Heavy rainfall over the Whitewater- Baldy burn scar in Catron County forced flood debris onto Bursum Road over a several mile stretch east of Mogollon.  Another storm around Edgewood washed out a portion of a roadway and caused damage to a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Barn flooded and damaged by running water on Brannan Road about 1 mile west of New Mexico Highway 217.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep pool of arctic air combined with a weak upper level low pressure system allowed widespread light to moderate snowfall to develop over northern and eastern New Mexico.  Persistent snowfall enhanced by upslope or low level east to west flow along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains produced significant snowfall and treacherous travel conditions along the Interstate 25 corridor between Raton Pass and Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 2 inch snowfall amounts with upward of 7 inches within the city of Raton.  Significant impacts to travel were noted along Interstate 25 around Raton with extreme caution advised by NMDOT.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving, moist upper level storm system pushed across New Mexico delivering strong winds along and east of the central mountain chain and heavy snow for portions of the northern mountains.  The first wave of this storm brought high elevation snow to the northern and western mountains and gusty southwest winds to central and eastern New Mexico.  The second wave of this storm was much colder and delivered widespread snowfall and gusty northwest winds.  The heaviest snowfall accumulations occurred over the northern mountains where the Sangre de Cristos picked up 12 to 24 inches of snow...particularly from the second wave of the system when cold moist westerly upslope flow impacted the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Combination of SNOTEL, public, and ski sites reported total snowfall accumulation of 12 to 18 inches along the west slopes of the Sangre de Cristo mountains. Cold temperatures and gusty winds created areas of blowing snow and hazardous travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across the NWS Albuquerque County Warning Area (CWA) as the new year began.||Much of the area received well below normal precipitation in January, as only three significant systems delivered moisture to parts of the area.  Northwest New Mexico, for example, received above normal precipitation with amounts 140-180% of normal. Other areas that were above normal for the month included the southwest mountains at 110-160% of normal, the eastern plains, ranging from 105-150% of normal, and some of the higher elevation areas of northern New Mexico where precipitation ranged from 107-140% of normal. The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.
A strong cutoff low moved over New Mexico and the Texas Panhandle, and resulted in blizzard conditions to the north of the upper low track and strong winds and blowing dust to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds of 40 mph or greater occurred for an hour at the Hobbs Mesonet site, 5 miles northwest of Hobbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low moved over New Mexico and the Texas Panhandle, and resulted in blizzard conditions to the north of the upper low track and strong winds and blowing dust to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds resulted in gusts over 60 mph at the Batdraw RAWS site in southwestern Eddy county.

The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair a weak slow moving upper level waves following in the wake of the winter storm system on the 20th and 21st produced a nearly continuous round of snowfall for the northern high terrain.  Areas above 9000 feet particularly at resort level picked up an additional 12 to 24 inches of new snow on top of the previous heavy snowfall from the 20-21st while lower terrain areas received an additional 4 to 8 inches.  The most significant impacts with this system were generally over the high terrain resort areas and had little impact on travel outside of high mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP, SNOTEL, and ski area reports indicated additional snowfall accumulations between 10 and 18 inches with continued travel impacts.

A deep upper level low pressure system pushed south along the California coastline on the 29th then weakened while turning slowly eastward across much of New Mexico on the 30th and 31st. Widespread light to moderate snowfall developed across many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Moderate to heavy snowfall developed over the northern mountains where 12 to 18 inches were reported. Very cold temperatures in association with this system created widespread hazardous travel conditions, particularly across the northern high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 10 inches were reported from COOP and SNOTEL data. Very cold temperatures created treacherous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system pushed south along the California coastline on the 29th then weakened while turning slowly eastward across much of New Mexico on the 30th and 31st. Widespread light to moderate snowfall developed across many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Moderate to heavy snowfall developed over the northern mountains where 12 to 18 inches were reported. Very cold temperatures in association with this system created widespread hazardous travel conditions, particularly across the northern high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 4 to 12 inches were reported from COOP, ski sites, and SNOTEL data. Very cold temperatures created hazardous travel conditions across this region.

Other areas that were above normal for the month included the southwest mountains at 110-160% of normal, the eastern plains, ranging from 105-150% of normal, and some of the higher elevation areas of northern New Mexico where precipitation ranged from 107-140% of normal. The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper level low pressure system pushed south along the California coastline on the 29th then weakened while turning slowly eastward across much of New Mexico on the 30th and 31st. Widespread light to moderate snowfall developed across many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Moderate to heavy snowfall developed over the northern mountains where 12 to 18 inches were reported. Very cold temperatures in association with this system created widespread hazardous travel conditions, particularly across the northern high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 3 to 15 inches were reported from SNOTEL, ski sites, and COOP data.  Very cold temperatures created hazardous travel conditions across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across the NWS Albuquerque County Warning Area (CWA) as the new year began.||Much of the area received well below normal precipitation in January, as only three significant systems delivered moisture to parts of the area.  Northwest New Mexico, for example, received above normal precipitation with amounts 140-180% of normal. Other areas that were above normal for the month included the southwest mountains at 110-160% of normal, the eastern plains, ranging from 105-150% of normal, and some of the higher elevation areas of northern New Mexico where precipitation ranged from 107-140% of normal. The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.

A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranging from 3-6 inches were reported across the zone. Several roads were closed across the area due to hazardous driving conditions.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds aloft combined with a deepening lee trough to produce widespread gusty surface winds over portions of the east slopes of the central mountain and the high plains.  An aircraft ascending from Angel Fire airport was blown back to the ground by wind gusts up to 55 mph resulting in 4 fatalities.  Elsewhere erratic wind gusts up to 60 mph impacted the area around Clines Corners.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust to 54mph at the Angel Fire airport lead to an aircraft accident with 4 fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranging from 5-12 inches were reported from co-op observers across the county with drifts measuring over one foot. Gusts into the fifties were recorded. NM DOT closed the main interstate that runs through this county as well as several side roads in the area due to the hazardous blizzard conditions. There was also a report of a power outage due to storm (Twitter) but can not be confirmed. Local news media reported 2 days after the event that an 81 year old man had died after he slipped on ice and hit his head while shoveling his driveway after the storm.

Other areas that were above normal for the month included the southwest mountains at 110-160% of normal, the eastern plains, ranging from 105-150% of normal, and some of the higher elevation areas of northern New Mexico where precipitation ranged from 107-140% of normal. The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.
A strong cutoff low moved over New Mexico and the Texas Panhandle, and resulted in blizzard conditions to the north of the upper low track and strong winds and blowing dust to the south.
A strong cutoff low moved over New Mexico and the Texas Panhandle, and resulted in blizzard conditions to the north of the upper low track and strong winds and blowing dust to the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northwest winds of 40 mph or greater occurred for an hour, 2 miles southwest of Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper level storm system sliding southeast out of the Great Basin interacted with a potent back door cold front sliding south down the Front Range to produce widespread heavy snowfall, strong to damaging winds, and blizzard to near blizzard conditions for much of eastern New Mexico. Widespread major impacts to travel developed across the eastern plains where nearly every U.S. and state highway as well as Interstates 25 and 40 were closed for several hours to even days. Snow and blowing snow produced drifts as high as 5 feet along the TX state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 2-4 inches were reported across the zone. Wind speeds sustained in the 30's with gusts into the 50's were also observed with this system. There were also some road closures in the area due to hazardous driving conditions in snow and blowing snow per NM DOT.

Strong winds developed over portions of south central and eastern NM as a potent upper level trough slammed eastward across the region early on the 17th.  Widespread windy conditions were reported with areas across the south central high terrain experiencing wind gusts as high as 70mph. Some damage was also reported around the central and northeast highlands as the potent cold front pushed through the region.  Widespread blowing dust occurred with this event...where some locales reported visibilities below one half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Double Eagle airport reported peak wind gusts between 40mph and 44mph for almost 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions persisted across the NWS Albuquerque County Warning Area (CWA).||Much of the area received below normal precipitation for the month of February. Areas in southern and southwest New Mexico, including the Upper Gila region, only received about 5-15% of normal precipitation for the month.  Across much of central, western, and northwest New Mexico,  percent of normal precipitation ranged from 25-50%. The eastern plains and the northeast corner of the state did fairly well thanks in large part to a major blizzard that impacted that part of the state during the last week of the month. ||Severe drought (D2) conditions were present rom the Sangre de Cristo range in the north to the Chuska Mountains in the west and south to the Upper Gila region. During the first two weeks of 2013, severe drought continued in the same areas as in January, but during the second half of February there was a drastic increase in extreme drought (D3) conditions.  D3 conditions expanded from mostly the eastern half of the state all the way west to near Gallup and to the southern extent of our CWA with only southwest Chaves County in D2 conditions. Exceptional drought (D4) conditions also expanded during the month. D4 continued in portions of Union and Roosevelt counties and began to develop in San Miguel County, inching into Guadalupe and Mora counties. The San Francisco River Valley in the southwest continued to stay out of D2 drought conditions. Also, portions of the far northwest highlands along the Colorado-New Mexico border began to ease out of D2 drought conditions.

A strong upper level trough along with a surface cold front combined to create high winds across much of New Mexico. As the Pacific storm system moved into portions of northwest New Mexico, winds increased across central and eastern areas. Snow fell throughout western portions of the state continuing across northern portions during the day.  A large area of strong winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere shifted from western to eastern New Mexico as the day progressed. The strongest winds prevailed along and east of the central mountain chain where gusts between 60-70kts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site recorded gusts up to 58mph during the late morning hours. Sustained wind speeds ranged from 40-45mph while gusts up to 58mph were recorded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions for March mimicked that of February. Sadly there was no improvement in conditions across the northern two-thirds of New Mexico but overall drought severity did not worsen. ||Precipitation was well-below normal areawide. Some areas in the southwest along with the northern mountains averaged 50-75% of normal while most other areas were only 10-25% of normal for the month. The eastern plains continued to experience only around 10% of normal precipitation values,  and the high plains of eastern New Mexico continue to be in the most severe category of drought in the state. March experienced numerous wind events with only one notable precipitation event, which occurred over the northern high terrain. Isolated severe weather mid-way through the month brought some precipitation to parts of southeast New Mexico, but wind was the main impact. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the area during March.  D2 conditions were present in the northwest corner of the state as well as from the Sangre de Cristo range in the north to the Chuska Mountains in the west and extending south to the southwest mountains.  During the first two weeks of March,  severe drought conditions persisted in the same general areas as in January and February, but during the second half of the month, there was a drastic increase in extreme drought (D3) conditions. D3 conditions expanded from being confined mostly to the eastern half of the state westward to near Gallup and to the southern extent of the area. Exceptional drought (D4) conditions also expanded during the month. D4 continued in portions of Union and Roosevelt counties and began to develop in San Miguel County, extending into Guadalupe and Mora counties. The San Francisco River Valley zone in the southwest continued to remain just below D2 drought status. Also, portions of the Far Northwest Highlands along the Colorado New Mexico border was unchanged for the month.

Widespread blowing dust occurred with this event...where some locales reported visibilities below one half mile at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sierra Blanca regional airport reported peak winds between 58mph and 69mph several times during the period.  The CWOP station 5 miles northwest of Nogal also reported high wind gusts to 58mph throughout this period.

The eastern plains continued to experience only around 10% of normal precipitation values,  and the high plains of eastern New Mexico continue to be in the most severe category of drought in the state. March experienced numerous wind events with only one notable precipitation event, which occurred over the northern high terrain. Isolated severe weather mid-way through the month brought some precipitation to parts of southeast New Mexico, but wind was the main impact. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the area during March.  D2 conditions were present in the northwest corner of the state as well as from the Sangre de Cristo range in the north to the Chuska Mountains in the west and extending south to the southwest mountains.  During the first two weeks of March,  severe drought conditions persisted in the same general areas as in January and February, but during the second half of the month, there was a drastic increase in extreme drought (D3) conditions. D3 conditions expanded from being confined mostly to the eastern half of the state westward to near Gallup and to the southern extent of the area. Exceptional drought (D4) conditions also expanded during the month. D4 continued in portions of Union and Roosevelt counties and began to develop in San Miguel County, extending into Guadalupe and Mora counties. The San Francisco River Valley zone in the southwest continued to remain just below D2 drought status. Also, portions of the Far Northwest Highlands along the Colorado New Mexico border was unchanged for the month.

A very strong backdoor cold front powered across the eastern plains of New Mexico during the late evening and overnight hours of April 22nd into April 23rd.  As the front continued southward the winds spread over the east central and southeast plains into the overnight hours. Most areas reached only wind advisory criteria while a couple sites located in the far northeast and east hit high wind speeds for a couple hours. Areas of reduced visibilities in blowing dust were also noted with this feature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tucumcari ASOS as well as Mesonet sites reported high sustained winds for about an hour during the early evening hours.

A strong upper level trough along with a surface cold front combined to create high winds across much of New Mexico. As the Pacific storm system moved into portions of northwest New Mexico, winds increased across central and eastern areas. Snow fell throughout western portions of the state continuing across northern portions during the day.  A large area of strong winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere shifted from western to eastern New Mexico as the day progressed. The strongest winds prevailed along and east of the central mountain chain where gusts between 60-70kts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 14 inches of new snow was reported at the Navajo Whiskey SNOTEL.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES OF THE SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft moved over northern and central New Mexico within the base of a large scale upper level trough over the western United States.  These winds mixed to the surface in localized areas along and immediately east of the northern mountains where high wind gusts were reported during the predawn hours through the early afternoon.  Elsewhere...widespread windy conditions were reported throughout the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12 inch diameter tree fell onto a car in Pecos, NM.  Little damage visible to vehicle.

Strong winds occurred in the Guadalupe Mountains and portions of the lower lands across Southeast New Mexico due to an upper trough moving through the southern Rocky Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained west to northwest winds of 40-45 mph, and gusts around 58 mph, occurred across portions of the Southeast New Mexico plains due to a strong upper level trough moving through the southern Rockies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance quickly moved east over New Mexico and produced widespread gusty dry showers and thunderstorms over central and western portions of the state.  Moisture and instability were sufficient to produce a band of severe thunderstorms over Curry and Roosevelt counties before the storms moved east into Texas.  Significant visibility reductions were reported across the eastern plains behind the line of thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Melrose range RAWS site reported peak wind gust to 60 mph.

Drought conditions persisted across the NWS Albuquerque County Warning Area (CWA) as the new year began.||Much of the area received well below normal precipitation in January, as only three significant systems delivered moisture to parts of the area.  Northwest New Mexico, for example, received above normal precipitation with amounts 140-180% of normal. Other areas that were above normal for the month included the southwest mountains at 110-160% of normal, the eastern plains, ranging from 105-150% of normal, and some of the higher elevation areas of northern New Mexico where precipitation ranged from 107-140% of normal. The remainder of the area continued previous trends of below to well-below normal precipitation varying from 14% of normal near Ocate and Socorro to 60% of normal near Clines Corners, Sumner Lake, El Morro, Mosquero and Wolf Canyon. ||Severe drought (D2) continued across most of the ABQ CWA. D2 conditions were present along central and western New Mexico,  including the far northwest highlands, San Juan Mountains, Jemez Mountains, Lower Rio Grande Valley and as far east as eastern Lincoln County.   Extreme (D3) drought was prevalent across much of the eastern plains from the central mountain chain eastward to include Colfax, Union, Mora, Harding, San Miguel, Guadalupe, De Baca, Quay, Curry, Roosevelt and portions of Chaves counties.  D3 drought was also present across portions of San Juan, McKinley and Cibola counties.  Toward the end of the month,  the drought status had improved to D2 status for the far northeast part of the state. Exceptional (D4) drought expanded across portions of Union, Curry and Roosevelt counties in the east.  Precipitation,  though not especially abundant,  precluded D2 drought status for the San Francisco River Valley in southwest New Mexico.

A strong upper level trough along with a surface cold front combined to create high winds across much of New Mexico. As the Pacific storm system moved into portions of northwest New Mexico, winds increased across central and eastern areas. Snow fell throughout western portions of the state continuing across northern portions during the day.  A large area of strong winds in the mid levels of the atmosphere shifted from western to eastern New Mexico as the day progressed. The strongest winds prevailed along and east of the central mountain chain where gusts between 60-70kts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS CQC at Clines Corners began recording sustained wind speeds of 40mph and gusts at and above 58mph during the late morning hours into the afternoon.

The strongest winds prevailed along and east of the central mountain chain where gusts between 60-70kts were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Sierra Blanca Airport in Ruidoso began recording sustained wind speeds of 40mph or above by early afternoon along with recorded gusts ranging from 55-70mph. This site was the big winner for the day with the highest recorded gust of 69mph.
