EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris were crossing the road along Highway 35 just north of Mimbres where Cottonwood Canyon and Mimbres River meet between mile marker 11 and 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level jet that oriented southwest to northeast over New Mexico interacted with deep moisture and surface heating to produce scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.  The first round of storms developed around the higher terrain of central New Mexico by early afternoon and pushed slowly into the Estancia Valley and nearby highlands.  A second round of storms with heavy rainfall developed well after sunset and pushed across the Manzano Mountains into the Estancia Valley again. Penny to half dollar size hail was reported with the first round of storms and flash flooding was reported with the second round.  A vehicle was washed off a road in Torrance County and its occupants required rescue by local law enforcement.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Vehicle stuck in water. Driver tried to cross flooded road or low water crossing on Spangler Rd. between Routes 55 and 542. Needed police assistance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms that developed over eastern New Mexico and western Texas pushed slowly south through the afternoon of the 15th.  High pressure aloft over western New Mexico provided north to south steering flow across the state.  One thunderstorm in particular deviated from the general north to south flow and moved southwest into Curry County from west Texas. This storm was particularly severe and produced funnel clouds and very large hail in the area from Pleasant Hill to Clovis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread quarter size hail reported over the entire east-side of Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed near the Santa Fe metro area drifted south and intensified around Golden during the early evening hours.  This storm complex produced heavy rainfall around Hyer Draw and resulted in flash flooding with road closures in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooded low water crossings resulting in road closures near intersection of NM 344 and NM 472.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, at times severe, thunderstorm moved across northern De Baca County. A 64 mph wind gust was recorded at the Ft. Sumner Municipal Airport, but no damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fort Sumner Airport reported 64 mph peak wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced torrential rainfall over Portales.  Local emergency management, law enforcement, and fire officials reported some residential flooding. Nearly 5000 sandbags were used to protect numerous homes. No homes were damaged, however, but photos of the area suggested some minor damage to landscaping. Curb-deep water did not subside in some of the hardest areas until the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media partner relayed report of hail ranging in size from marble to golf ball in association with a passing storm near Moriarty.  One half an inch of rain was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance pushed southeast across northern New Mexico and interacted with a stationary frontal boundary in the Rio Grande Valley.  The combination of abundant moisture and instability and northwest flow aloft produced repeated rounds of heavy rainfall across areas in northern as well as central portions of the state including the Las Conchas burn scar and the Albuquerque Metro Area. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches were reported with these storms. Flooding initially occurred in north-central NM during the afternoon and evening hours before training cells developed in northern Bernalillo County during the overnight hours. The Interstate 25 corridor around Bernalillo into north Albuquerque was one of the hardest hit. Widespread flooding was reported in Bernalillo due to the heavy rainfall and a breached acequia. Up to 5 feet of water was reported in one home in Bernalillo.  Frequent cloud to ground lightning strikes created numerous power outages across Santa Fe with nearly 30,000 customers out in Albuquerque. Lightning was reported to have hit Sandia Casino in northeast Albuquerque shortly after midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained Spotter relayed video of flooding beneath a prominent underpass in north Raton. A woman drove her car with 2 children into water and became submerged and had to be rescued by officials. Water was reported to be 11-12ft deep. Flooding occurred in an underpass where 2nd St. in north Raton crosses the railroad tracks. Spotter reported a total rainfall amount of 1.47 inches with 1.22 inches with the initial burst and an inch of that falling in 30 minutes. Raton Creek was reported to be 7-8ft deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper high was centered over the Great Basin and continued north to south flow aloft over New Mexico. Storms developed during the mid afternoon hours and tracked southward throughout the day into the evening. Slow storm motions led to flooding across the Whitewater baldy burn scar in southwestern New Mexico.||An Albuquerque couple was struck by lightning while watching Fourth of July fireworks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On July 4th, during a torrential storm, Engine 12 and Rescue 12 of the Albuquerque Fire Dept. were near the area of Eubank and Copper when they heard a very loud lightning strike. Approximately 60-90 seconds later, Engine 12 was dispatched to a residence where a tree had been struck.  Two individuals were injured including a pregnant woman who was two weeks away from her due date.  The 38- week pregnant female and her boyfriend were assessed, treated and transported to a local hospital. The woman underwent an emergency C-section. Days later, mom and dad along with their new baby girl were released from the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge shifted eastward toward southwestern New Mexico in response to a short wave trough moving into the Pacific Northwest. A moist and unstable airmass over New Mexico led to an uptick in shower and thunderstorm development throughout the day. Storms pushed east-southeast off the higher terrain producing some locally heavy rainfall and gusty winds.  One storm in particular that impacted areas near Los Alamos produced strong winds. Additionally, heavy rain from a cluster of storms along U.S. 491 in San Juan County produced significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site recorded a 58mph wind gust as an isolated cell moved from NW to the SE across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local news station reported that heavy rains Friday night caused part of U.S. 491 to collapse creating a sinkhole. The rain was reported to have damaged a culvert that ran underneath the roadway leading to the collapse. U.S. 491 was closed in both directions between Sheep Springs and Naschitti for several days after the event.

A deep northerly flow was setup across the region as a deep upper low was located over the Mississippi River Valley and an upper ridge over the Great Basin. A back door cold front moved into the Rio Grande Valley bringing in some low level moisture and providing the focus for severe thunderstorms to develop across the region. Wind gusts up to 90 mph were found to occur after a damage survey team assessed damage around the Elephant Butte Lake area. Some flash flooding was also reported near the Silver Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator in Deming estimated wind gusts to 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level jet combined with sufficient low level moisture and instability to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico.  Slow-moving thunderstorms that developed over western New Mexico produced flash flooding in portions of Cibola and McKinley counties where several roads were washed out or closed.  Severe hail also accompanied these storms and as much as 8 inches of rain accumulated along portions of the roadway near the Bandera Ice Caves.  As these storms moved east, they continued to produce sporadic large hail reports including areas around Santa Fe. Another round of thunderstorms developed after sunset over western Catron County and produced a significant flash flood wave in Vigil Canyon where debris was found 15 feet into the trees the following day.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported that roads were closed in Onava due to flooding. Reports from broadcast media also indicated that the Interstate 25 frontage road was washed over between the Las Vegas Airport and the Onava exit. Extensive damage also occurred to guardrails and a bridge located on CR-23. The flooding appeared to be the result of heavy rainfall that fell within the Pecos Arroyo farther upstream where the first flooding event occurred.

A slow moving complex of thunderstorms that developed along the east slopes of the Jemez Mountains produced heavy rainfall, gusty winds, and a funnel cloud over the Los Alamos area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud developed just to the southwest of White Rock.  A photo taken by Karl Maness showed a well developed funnel as viewed from the LANL Emergency Operations Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed just north of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the early afternoon and within an hour, isolated strong thunderstorms were directly over the burn scar that caused flooding in local creeks. Similarly, isolated strong storms developed in north central NM over a second burn scar where flooding was also observed during the evening hours.  Later in the day,  a large complex of strong thunderstorms organized over north central New Mexico then slowly pushed south along the entire Rio Grande Valley into the Big Bend region of Texas. As this storm complex pushed into the Albuquerque Metro Area, a severe downburst wind measuring a historical 89mph at the Sunport surged out ahead of the storm and produced extensive damage and flash flooding to many areas along and south of Interstate 40. Several outdoor events were severely impacted, including an Albuquerque Isotopes baseball game, Summer Fest at the BioPark Zoo, and a concert at the Isleta Ampitheater. Downed tree branches and uprooted trees created extensive power outages leaving more than 25,000 customers without power. Interstate 25 was closed between the Big I and Rio Bravo Boulevard for nearly 12 hours as downed power lines and power poles were repaired along several access ramps.  Flash flooding with these thunderstorms stranded several motorists in several feet of water across downtown Albuquerque and in many other areas of town.  This complex of storms continued to produce flash flooding as it moved south through Los Lunas, Belen, and much of Socorro County.  Flooding at a waste water treatment facility in Belen prompted a state of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to the doors of vehicles on Alameda westbound from Rio Grande Rd. to Coors. Report of a truck stuck in the water.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bland and Peralta Canyons flooded across low water crossings and was described as a flash flood situation by Cochiti Pueblo emergency management. Water came up to Cochiti highway but did not top it. Bandelier National Monument also received flooding Thursday night into Friday morning with mud and debris being deposited across parking lots and bridges in the area. The Monument had to stay closed Friday due to continued clean up efforts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high continued to dominate the weather pattern over New Mexico. Most of the state was under weak northwest to southeast flow aloft as the upper high was centered generally over southwest New Mexico. A weak ripple embedded in this flow assisted in the development of isolated showers and thunderstorms throughout the day. Slow storm motions allowed individual storms to remain over locations for long periods of time, sometimes over an hour, with impressive radar estimated storm total precipitation values ranging from 2-4. One storm in particular produced flash flooding in Santa Fe County as well as over a recent burn scar, Tres Lagunas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: BAER team on the Tres Lagunas Burn Scar reported flooding in the area. Minor flooding was reported across NM 63 where sediment and small rocks were carried over a portion of the road between the Windy Bridge and Tererro areas in the Santa Fe National Forest. Major flooding was reported over Forest Road 122 a.k.a. Holy Ghost, which was impassable after large rocks were rolled over the road by flood waters. Storms developed and stayed over the burn location for close to an hour with light rain persisting another half hour and additional light rain about 6 hours later.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that heavy rains had washed out roads in his neighborhood. His neighbor called to relay that 3 inches of rain fell in an hour causing major flooding on roads. He later reported that the culvert into an arroyo could not handle the volume of water and washed out Deer Tank Rd. Deer Tank Rd. was impassable for much of the evening and next morning. Water on roads of 3ft deep and 50-60ft wide were reported (all dirt roads). Radar showed an isolated thunderstorm over the area that stayed in the general vicinity for over an hour. Highest reflectivity was 66dBz. Storm total precipitation estimates had numerous values greater than 3.5.

Some water made it up the emergency spillway and ran into the pond and then into the Corrales Main Canal, causing large erosions in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm that impacted Gallup produced flash flooding across several areas of town.  A juvenile detention center reported 6 inches of water on the ground floor.  The combination of heavy rain and hail created flooding impacts at the Red Rock Care Center where damage was reported to the dining and residence halls.  County Road 5 was also impacted as heavy flows through an arroyo topped this low water crossing bridge by as much as 5 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water flooded the ground floor of the McKinley County Juvenile Detention Center.

Wind gusts up to 90 mph were found to occur after a damage survey team assessed damage around the Elephant Butte Lake area. Some flash flooding was also reported near the Silver Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 12 RV trailers were blown over and flipped in Elephant Butte State Park between Hot Springs Landing and Rock Canyon Marina. Some were displaced 30 to 40 yards and a few even went airborne. Winds of more than 70 mph were measured at Marina Del Sur. Damage survey assessment team found damage indicative of 80 to 90 mph. Several people in the town of Elephant Butte lost their porches and had a lot of roof damage as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep northerly flow was setup across the region as a deep upper low was located over the Mississippi River Valley and an upper ridge over the Great Basin. A back door cold front moved into the Rio Grande Valley bringing in some low level moisture and providing the focus for severe thunderstorms to develop across the region. Wind gusts up to 90 mph were found to occur after a damage survey team assessed damage around the Elephant Butte Lake area. Some flash flooding was also reported near the Silver Fire burn scar.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated around 60 mph by a NWS Employee near the Hatch exit of Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong upper level jet combined with sufficient low level moisture and instability to produce several areas of strong to severe thunderstorms over central and western New Mexico.  Slow-moving thunderstorms that developed over western New Mexico produced flash flooding in portions of Cibola and McKinley counties where several roads were washed out or closed.  Severe hail also accompanied these storms and as much as 8 inches of rain accumulated along portions of the roadway near the Bandera Ice Caves.  As these storms moved east, they continued to produce sporadic large hail reports including areas around Santa Fe. Another round of thunderstorms developed after sunset over western Catron County and produced a significant flash flood wave in Vigil Canyon where debris was found 15 feet into the trees the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported just north of Bluewater Lake near Mt. Taylor in the vicinity of two merging thunderstorm cells.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain was reported with afternoon storms with an additional inch during the evening and overnight hours. Non-paved roads in the area were all undercut and washed out with large boulders blocking portions of the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system over the Four Corners region interacted with a surface frontal boundary draped over northeastern New Mexico to produce strong to severe thunderstorms over Colfax and Union counties.  The strongest storms produced gusty winds and nickel to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee from neighboring office was hiking in the area and employees from the Philmont Scout Ranch reported hail the size of golfballs associated with this storm. Numerous tree branches downed across the ranch property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was located across Southwest New Mexico with a weak mid level trough off to the southwest. A deep layer of moisture was in place across the region with a wet microburst reported in Columbus, NM with winds of up to 90 mph reported. In addition quarter sized hail was reported around Hurley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report to 911 said there was at least quarter sized hail near the entrance to mine near Hurley and that windshield was cracked due to the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture combined with weak steering winds aloft set the stage for an active crop of slow-moving thunderstorms across much of central and western New Mexico during the afternoon and evening.  Several strong thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall and accumulating hail in the vicinity of Grants and the Acoma Pueblo.  The storms resulted in significant impacts to the Interstate 40 corridor just east of Grants.  Rainfall amounts between 1 and 3 inches were reported.  Pea-sized hail accumulated up to a couple of inches near McCartys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall lead to ponding of water 6 to 8 inches deep along Interstate 40 east and west bound near McCartys.  Numerous accidents were reported in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, slow-moving thunderstorms produced very heavy rain and pea-sized hail over the western portion of the Whitewater-Baldy burn scar during the late afternoon hours. Rainfall rates just under two inches per hour were reported.  Delayed runoff from the heavy rain resulted in rapid rises of Whitewater Creek near and downstream of the Catwalk after sunset while flash flooding closed portions of NM 174.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff resulted in flash flooding along Whitewater Creek with a report of ���a roaring��� sound near the Deer Park drainage channel. Black and silty water was reported along with considerable debris across NM 174. Up to 14 inches of water was flowing over the highway at the upper crossing just downstream of the Catwalk.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee reported some water crossing the highway near the intersection of Highway 35 and Highway 152 near San Lorenzo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ranger at the El Malpais National Monument reported funnel clouds near La Ventana arch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dew points around 60 at the surface combined with weak southwest flow aloft ahead of a west coast trough provided ample instability and shear for training storms to develop over Northern Sierra County and produce flash flooding along Alamosa Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newspaper report on social media showed flooding across Highway 142 at bridge on Alamosa Creek in Monticello Canyon.  The rain fell upstream of the location near the Sierra-Socorro County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was located along the Rio Grande Valley with an upper level trough located near the Arizona-New Mexico border. Deep moisture remained in place allowing for heavy rain and flash flooding to occur. Winds up to 70 mph were also reported near Las Cruces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports from 911 said there was a swift water rescue on the corner of Camino Lindo and Sonoma Ranch Blvd in Las Cruces. Flooding was also reported at Sierra Middle School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was located along the Rio Grande Valley with an upper level trough located near the Arizona-New Mexico border. Deep moisture remained in place allowing for heavy rain and flash flooding to occur. Winds up to 70 mph were also reported near Las Cruces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Mesonet site near Talvera recorded a 70 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary was located along the Rio Grande Valley with an upper level trough located near the Arizona-New Mexico border. Deep moisture remained in place allowing for heavy rain and flash flooding to occur. Winds up to 70 mph were also reported near Las Cruces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS employee reported a tree down in downtown Las Cruces.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain was reported during the afternoon with an additional one inch of rain during the night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Nevada maintained a persistent northerly flow over New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the peaks of the northern high terrain and drifted south throughout the day as storms increased in both coverage and intensity. Some of the storms became severe as vertical wind shear was more than sufficient to support organized storms. The main threat was gusty outflow winds, especially for western New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported torrents of water along NM 3 with up to 4 feet of flowing water over the road.  Considerable hail and reduced visibilities also accompanied the storms. Difficult driving conditions due to heavy rain,  hail and near zero visibility were also reported along Interstate 40 in this area via NMDOT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined upper level shortwave diving south out of the central Rockies interacted with deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Severe outflow winds, localized wind damage, large hail, locally heavy rainfall, and significant blowing dust were reported with some of these storms. The most severe thunderstorms produced baseball size hail around Arabela and damaging wind in excess of 70mph around Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseball hail 6 miles north of Highway 246.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moisture rich airmass was in place along and east of the central mountain chain. With the increased moisture and adequate instability some isolated severe storms developed in southern Colorado and migrated to the southeast into northeast New Mexico. Some storms also developed over the the Sangre de Cristo mountains and these migrated to the east into the northeast highlands throughout the afternoon into evening hours. A series of long track severe storms in particular impacted a narrow region with severe hail from near Raton southeastward across the plains to south of Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball hail 13 miles northwest of Sedan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dryline across the eastern plains of New Mexico provided a focus for several severe thunderstorms by late afternoon.  Surface dewpoints to the east of the dryline were in the lower to middle 60s while dewpoints in the single digits were pushing in from the west.  The main focus for severe weather occurred along the dryline from near Tucumcari south to around Fort Sumner and east of the Roswell area.  Numerous reports of large hail, strong gusty winds, and hazardous blowing dust were reported, along with a funnel cloud a few miles north of Tucumcari.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel hail 29 miles south of Yeso.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Vigil Canyon near Alma was reported by two rangers that were trapped and nearly carried away by flood waters. Flood debris was reported 15 feet in depth within the canyon. Some elk fences were destroyed and several mobile home trailers were briefly endangered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A convectively-induced back door frontal boundary shifted slowly southwest across eastern New Mexico beneath northwest flow aloft.  Deep moisture and afternoon heating spawned several strong thunderstorms along this boundary. Strong winds were reported at the Tucumcari airport with numerous reports of heavy rainfall, intense lightning, and pea to quarter size hail.  Thunderstorms impacted Interstate 40 between Tucumcari and San Jon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported pea to quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well defined upper level shortwave diving south out of the central Rockies interacted with deep moisture and instability over eastern New Mexico to produce several severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours.  Severe outflow winds, localized wind damage, large hail, locally heavy rainfall, and significant blowing dust were reported with some of these storms. The most severe thunderstorms produced baseball size hail around Arabela and damaging wind in excess of 70mph around Curry and Roosevelt counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar hail in Ocate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnant moisture was recycled under the upper level high throughout the afternoon and evening hours. Storms began to develop in the northeast and the southwest during the early morning hours before new development commenced in the northern high terrain. With the upper level high shifting towards the west,  storm motions across the northern half of the state were to the south while storms in the south were to the north, both under 10 mph. Coverage increased into the evening hours across the north and east becoming scattered to numerous. A large swath of precipitation moved down eastern New Mexico with thunderstorm cells also developing around the Albuquerque area causing flooding from heavy downpours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported that Unser Rd. was closed between St. Josephs and Vista Oriente due to flooding. Rainfall amounts of over one inch in 20 minutes were reported in the area. Power was out for approximately 10,000 customers in association with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and weak instability present across the eastern plains combined with afternoon heating to produce scattered showers and thunderstorms.  A few of these thunderstorms became locally severe with large hail and gusty winds.  Virga descending from thunderstorm anvils spreading over Roswell produced wind gusts up to 70 mph and significant blowing dust.  Heavy rainfall was also reported with these storms across the northeast plains. Accumulating hail along Interstate 25 near Highway 64 created a rollover accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixture of golf ball and pea size hail reported at the NRA Whittington Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dryline was located across the Permian Basin and the Trans Pecos, and a cold front was in the Texas panhandle.  Good lower level moisture was present to the east of the dryline, and there was plentiful upper level moisture available. The atmosphere was very unstable.  Mid-level lift was present with an upper level disturbance that moved over the area.  This combination of moisture, lift, and instability helped to create several severe thunderstorms that produced large hail and damaging winds across Southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm moved over Eddy County and produced an 80 mph wind gust which peeled back the roof of the VFW in Carlsbad.  The cost of damage is a rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving thunderstorm produced torrential rainfall over Portales.  Local emergency management, law enforcement, and fire officials reported some residential flooding. Nearly 5000 sandbags were used to protect numerous homes. No homes were damaged, however, but photos of the area suggested some minor damage to landscaping. Curb-deep water did not subside in some of the hardest areas until the following day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported around Portales High School.  Numerous sandbags and resources deployed to prevent significant residential flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level jet axis focused directly over central New Mexico interacted with abundant low level moisture entrenched over the region to produce scattered strong to severe thunderstorms.  These storms were slow-moving and in many cases trained over the same areas repeatedly through the afternoon and early evening hours.  Heavy rainfall with this convective activity fell on already saturated soil conditions and exacerbated flooding concerns after a historic week of flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video sent in from observer showed a well-defined gustnado that briefly spun up into a landspout tornado for a few minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed near the Santa Fe metro area drifted south and intensified around Golden during the early evening hours.  This storm complex produced heavy rainfall around Hyer Draw and resulted in flash flooding with road closures in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff exceeding bank full in Hyer Draw. Water running out of arroyo and across low spots on the southeast corner of spotters 10 acre plot of land.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft centered over Nevada maintained a persistent northerly flow over New Mexico. Showers and thunderstorms initially developed over the peaks of the northern high terrain and drifted south throughout the day as storms increased in both coverage and intensity. Some of the storms became severe as vertical wind shear was more than sufficient to support organized storms. The main threat was gusty outflow winds, especially for western New Mexico.

Upper level high pressure centered over the Great Basin continued a north to south flow aloft over New Mexico. Sufficient moisture in place across the area fueled thunderstorm development along the eastern periphery of the upper high. Storms initially developed across the northern high terrain before moving south and then southwest. Isolated strong to severe storms developed in the afternoon with numerous reports of large hail and severe winds and minor road flooding along with copious amounts of mainly small hail across the eastern plains.  Among the most severe storms struck Santa Rosa, New Mexico.  Nickel to golfball-sized hail fell in the Santa Rosa area resulting in major damage. The smaller hail accumulated up two feet deep in some spots and snow plows were used to clear roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Residents were reported to be stranded in the Meadow Lake and El Cerro Mission areas east of Los Lunas due to flooding. Roads were completely covered with water and pumps were required for water removal. Some residents reported water several feet deep. Much of the impacted area has non-paved roads and runoff originated from heavy rains over the Manzano Mountains immediately to the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level short wave trough combined with sufficient low level moisture and strong instability to produce isolated severe thunderstorms stretching from De Baca County northeast to Union County.  Reports of half dollar to ping pong ball size hail were received around the Yeso area with damage to trees.  Heavy rainfall was also reported with these storms.  No reports were found of severe weather in southern Union county however radar indicated very strong signatures for severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fifteen to 20 minute period of hail with largest stones ranging from half dollar to ping pong ball size near Yeso.  Leaves were stripped off trees but no significant damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The center of upper level high pressure was located near Farmington in northwestern New Mexico. Plentiful moisture and a general south or southeast surface flow fueled daily rounds of showers and thunderstorms. Most of the cells stayed fairly tame except for one line of storms that developed during the evening hours right on the Socorro County/Sierra County line. This line of thunderstorms moved southwest with a single strong gust being reported as the storms moved over reported location.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 61 mph was recorded from a 10-meter White Sands Missile Range mission support tower in extreme southern Socorro County.
